title: Aeneid, Book 7
source: Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th c.) via Vatican Library IIIF + Perseus Digital Library Latin (Greenough ed.) + Dryden English (1697, PG #228)
description: Book 7 of Virgil's Aeneid. Manuscript folio from Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225), the oldest surviving illustrated manuscript of Virgil (4th-5th century AD), held at the Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. Latin text verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain). English translation verbatim from John Dryden's verse rendering (1697, Project Gutenberg #228, public domain).
introduction:
This entry presents Book 7 of Virgil's Aeneid with the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: A folio from the Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th century), one of the oldest and most important surviving Virgil manuscripts, with painted illustrations in the late antique Roman style. Via Vatican Library IIIF (digi.vatlib.it).
  2. Original text: The complete Latin text of Aeneid Book 7 (817 lines), verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain).
  3. English translation: John Dryden's celebrated verse translation (1697), verbatim from Project Gutenberg #228 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>Tu quoque litoribus nostris, Aeneia nutrix, <sup>2</sup>aeternam moriens famam, Caieta, dedisti; <sup>3</sup>et nunc servat honos sedem tuus ossaque nomen <sup>4</sup>Hesperia in magna, siqua est ea gloria, signat. <sup>5</sup>At pius exsequiis Aeneas rite solutis, <sup>6</sup>aggere composito tumuli, postquam alta quierunt <sup>7</sup>aequora, tendit iter velis portumque relinquit. <sup>8</sup>Adspirant aurae in noctem nec candida cursus <sup>9</sup>Luna negat, splendet tremulo sub lumine pontus. <sup>10</sup>Proxima Circaeae raduntur litora terrae, <sup>11</sup>dives inaccessos ubi Solis filia lucos <sup>12</sup>adsiduo resonat cantu tectisque superbis <sup>13</sup>urit odoratam nocturna in lumina cedrum, <sup>14</sup>arguto tenuis percurrens pectine telas. <sup>15</sup>Hinc exaudiri gemitus iraeque leonum <sup>16</sup>vincla recusantum et sera sub nocte rudentum, <sup>17</sup>saetigerique sues atque in praesaepibus ursi <sup>18</sup>saevire ac formae magnorum ululare luporum, <sup>19</sup>quos hominum ex facie dea saeva potentibus herbis <sup>20</sup>induerat Circe in voltus ac terga ferarum. <sup>21</sup>Quae ne monstra pii paterentur talia Troes <sup>22</sup>delati in portus neu litora dira subirent, <sup>23</sup>Neptunus ventis implevit vela secundis <sup>24</sup>atque fugam dedit et praeter vada fervida vexit. <sup>25</sup>Iamque rubescebat radiis mare et aethere ab alto
translation.text: King Latinus entertains Aeneas, and promises him his only daughter, Lavinia, the heiress of his crown. Turnus, being in love with her, favoured by her mother, and by Juno and Alecto, breaks the treaty which was made, and engages in his quarrel Mezentius, Camilla, Messapus, and many other of the neighbouring princes; whose forces, and the names of their commanders are particularly related.

translation.original_text: <sup>26</sup>Aurora in roseis fulgebat lutea bigis: <sup>27</sup>cum venti posuere omnisque repente resedit <sup>28</sup>flatus et in lento luctantur marmore tonsae. <sup>29</sup>Atque hic Aeneas ingentem ex aequore lucum <sup>30</sup>prospicit. Hunc inter fluvio Tiberinus amoeno <sup>31</sup>verticibus rapidis et multa flavus arena <sup>32</sup>in mare prorumpit. Variae circumque supraque <sup>33</sup>adsuetae ripis volucres et fluminis alveo <sup>34</sup>aethera mulcebant cantu lucoque volabant. <sup>35</sup>flectere iter sociis terraeque advertere proras <sup>36</sup>imperat et laetus fluvio succedit opaco. <sup>37</sup>Nunc age, qui reges, Erato, quae tempora rerum, <sup>38</sup>quis Latio antiquo fuerit status, advena classem <sup>39</sup>cum primum Ausoniis exercitus appulit oris, <sup>40</sup>expediam et primae revocabo exordia pugnae. <sup>41</sup>tu vatem, tu, diva, mone. Dicam horrida bella, <sup>42</sup>dicam acies actosque animis in funera reges <sup>43</sup>Tyrrhenamque manum totamque sub arma coactam <sup>44</sup>Hesperiam. Maior rerum mihi nascitur ordo, <sup>45</sup>maius opus moveo.Rex arva Latinus et urbes <sup>46</sup>iam senior longa placidas in pace regebat. <sup>47</sup>Hunc Fauno et nympha genitum Laurente Marica <sup>48</sup>accipimus, Fauno Picus pater isque parentem <sup>49</sup>te, Saturne, refert, tu sanguinis ultimus auctor. <sup>50</sup>filius huic fato divom prolesque virilis
translation.text: And thou, O matron of immortal fame, Here dying, to the shore hast left thy name; Cajeta still the place is call’d from thee, The nurse of great Aeneas’ infancy. Here rest thy bones in rich Hesperia’s plains; Thy name (’tis all a ghost can have) remains.

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>nulla fuit primaque oriens erepta iuventa est. <sup>52</sup>Sola domum et tantas servabat filia sedes, <sup>53</sup>iam matura viro, iam plenis nubilis annis. <sup>54</sup>Multi illam magno e Latio totaque petebant <sup>55</sup>Ausonia. Petit ante alios pulcherrimus omnis <sup>56</sup>Turnus, avis atavisque potens, quem regia coniunx <sup>57</sup>adiungi generum miro properabat amore; <sup>58</sup>sed variis portenta deum terroribus obstant. <sup>59</sup>Laurus erat tecti medio in penetralibus altis, <sup>60</sup>sacra comam multosque metu servata per annos, <sup>61</sup>quam pater inventam, primas cum conderet arces, <sup>62</sup>ipse ferebatur Phoebo sacrasse Latinus <sup>63</sup>Laurentisque ab ea nomen posuisse colonis. <sup>64</sup>Huius apes summum densae (mirabile dictu), <sup>65</sup>stridore ingenti liquidum trans aethera vectae, <sup>66</sup>obsedere apicem, ex pedibus per mutua nexis <sup>67</sup>examen subitum ramo frondente pependit. <sup>68</sup>Continuo vates: Externum cernimus, inquit, <sup>69</sup>adventare virum et partis petere agmen easdem <sup>70</sup>partibus ex isdem et summa dominarier arce. <sup>71</sup>Praeterea, castis adolet dum altaria taedis <sup>72</sup>et iuxta genitorem adstat Lavinia virgo, <sup>73</sup>visa (nefas) longis comprendere crinibus ignem, <sup>74</sup>atque omnem ornatum flamma crepitante cremari <sup>75</sup>regalisque accensa comas, accensa coronam
translation.text:
Now, when the prince her fun’ral rites had paid, He plow’d the Tyrrhene seas with sails display’d. From land a gentle breeze arose by night, Serenely shone the stars, the moon was bright, And the sea trembled with her silver light. Now near the shelves of Circe’s shores they run, (Circe the rich, the daughter of the Sun,) A dang’rous coast: the goddess wastes her days In joyous songs; the rocks resound her lays: In spinning, or the loom, she spends the night, And cedar brands supply her father’s light. From hence were heard, rebellowing to the main, The roars of lions that refuse the chain, The grunts of bristled boars, and groans of bears, And herds of howling wolves that stun the sailors’ ears. These from their caverns, at the close of night, Fill the sad isle with horror and affright. Darkling they mourn their fate, whom Circe’s pow’r, (That watch’d the moon and planetary hour,) With words and wicked herbs from humankind Had alter’d, and in brutal shapes confin’d. Which monsters lest the Trojans’ pious host Should bear, or touch upon th’ inchanted coast, Propitious Neptune steer’d their course by night With rising gales that sped their happy flight. Supplied with these, they skim the sounding shore, And hear the swelling surges vainly roar. Now, when the rosy morn began to rise, And wav’d her saffron streamer thro’ the skies; When Thetis blush’d in purple not her own, And from her face the breathing winds were blown, A sudden silence sate upon the sea, And sweeping oars, with struggling, urge their way. The Trojan, from the main, beheld a wood, Which thick with shades and a brown horror stood: Betwixt the trees the Tiber took his course, With whirlpools dimpled; and with downward force, That drove the sand along, he took his way, And roll’d his yellow billows to the sea. About him, and above, and round the wood, The birds that haunt the borders of his flood, That bath’d within, or basked upon his side, To tuneful songs their narrow throats applied. The captain gives command; the joyful train Glide thro’ the gloomy shade, and leave the main.

Now, Erato, thy poet’s mind inspire, And fill his soul with thy celestial fire! Relate what Latium was; her ancient kings; Declare the past and present state of things, When first the Trojan fleet Ausonia sought, And how the rivals lov’d, and how they fought. These are my theme, and how the war began, And how concluded by the godlike man: For I shall sing of battles, blood, and rage, Which princes and their people did engage; And haughty souls, that, mov’d with mutual hate, In fighting fields pursued and found their fate; That rous’d the Tyrrhene realm with loud alarms, And peaceful Italy involv’d in arms. A larger scene of action is display’d; And, rising hence, a greater work is weigh’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>76</sup>insignem gemmis, tum fumida lumine fulvo <sup>77</sup>involvi ac totis Volcanum spargere tectis. <sup>78</sup>Id vero horrendum ac visu mirabile ferri: <sup>79</sup>namque fore inlustrem fama fatisque canebant <sup>80</sup>ipsam, sed populo magnum portendere bellum. <sup>81</sup>At rex sollicitus monstris oracula Fauni, <sup>82</sup>fatidici genitoris, adit lucosque sub alta <sup>83</sup>consulit Albunea, nemorum quae maxima sacro <sup>84</sup>fonte sonat saevamque exhalat opaca mephitim. <sup>85</sup>Hinc Italae gentes omnisque Oenotria tellus <sup>86</sup>in dubiis responsa petunt; huc dona sacerdos <sup>87</sup>cum tulit et caesarum ovium sub nocte silenti <sup>88</sup>pellibus incubuit stratis somnosque petivit, <sup>89</sup>multa modis simulacra videt volitantia miris <sup>90</sup>et varias audit voces fruiturque deorum <sup>91</sup>conloquio atque imis Acheronta adfatur Avernis. <sup>92</sup>Hic et tum pater ipse petens responsa Latinus <sup>93</sup>centum lanigeras mactabat rite bidentis <sup>94</sup>atque harum effultus tergo stratisque iacebat <sup>95</sup>velleribus: subita ex alto vox reddita luco est: <sup>96</sup>Ne pete conubiis natam sociare Latinis, <sup>97</sup>O mea progenies, thalamis neu crede paratis: <sup>98</sup>externi venient generi, qui sanguine nostrum <sup>99</sup>nomen in astra ferant quorumque a stirpe nepotes <sup>100</sup>omnia sub pedibus, qua Sol utrumque recurrens
translation.text: Latinus, old and mild, had long possess’d The Latin scepter, and his people blest: His father Faunus; a Laurentian dame His mother; fair Marica was her name. But Faunus came from Picus: Picus drew His birth from Saturn, if records be true. Thus King Latinus, in the third degree, Had Saturn author of his family. But this old peaceful prince, as Heav’n decreed, Was blest with no male issue to succeed: His sons in blooming youth were snatch’d by fate; One only daughter heir’d the royal state. Fir’d with her love, and with ambition led, The neighb’ring princes court her nuptial bed. Among the crowd, but far above the rest, Young Turnus to the beauteous maid address’d. Turnus, for high descent and graceful mien, Was first, and favour’d by the Latian queen; With him she strove to join Lavinia’s hand, But dire portents the purpos’d match withstand.

translation.original_text: <sup>101</sup>aspicit Oceanum, vertique regique videbunt. <sup>102</sup>Haec responsa patris Fauni monitusque silenti <sup>103</sup>nocte datos non ipse suo premit ore Latinus, <sup>104</sup>sed circum late volitans iam Fama per urbes <sup>105</sup>Ausonias tulerat, cum Laomedontia pubes <sup>106</sup>gramineo ripae religavit ab aggere classem. <sup>107</sup>Aeneas primique duces et pulcher Iulus <sup>108</sup>corpora sub ramis deponunt arboris altae <sup>109</sup>instituuntque dapes et adorea liba per herbam <sup>110</sup>subiciunt epulis (sic Iuppiter ipse monebat) <sup>111</sup>et Cereale solum pomis agrestibus augent. <sup>112</sup>Consumptis hic forte aliis ut vertere morsus <sup>113</sup>exiguam in Cererem penuria adegit edendi <sup>114</sup>et violare manu malisque audacibus orbem <sup>115</sup>fatalis crusti patulis nec parcere quadris: <sup>116</sup>Heus ! etiam mensas consumimus, inquit Iulus, <sup>117</sup>nec plura adludens. Ea vox audita laborum <sup>118</sup>prima tulit finem, primamque loquentis ab ore <sup>119</sup>eripuit pater ac stupefactus numine pressit. <sup>120</sup>Continuo: Salve fatis mihi debita tellus <sup>121</sup>vosque, ait, O fidi Troiae salvete penates: <sup>122</sup>hic domus, haec patria est. Genitor mihi talia namque <sup>123</sup>(nunc repeto) Anchises fatorum arcana reliquit: <sup>124</sup>cum te, nate, fames ignota ad litora vectum <sup>125</sup>accisis coget dapibus consumere mensas,
translation.text:
Deep in the palace, of long growth, there stood A laurel’s trunk, a venerable wood; Where rites divine were paid; whose holy hair Was kept and cut with superstitious care. This plant Latinus, when his town he wall’d, Then found, and from the tree Laurentum call’d; And last, in honour of his new abode, He vow’d the laurel to the laurel’s god. It happen’d once (a boding prodigy!) A swarm of bees, that cut the liquid sky, Unknown from whence they took their airy flight, Upon the topmost branch in clouds alight; There with their clasping feet together clung, And a long cluster from the laurel hung. An ancient augur prophesied from hence: “Behold on Latian shores a foreign prince! From the same parts of heav’n his navy stands, To the same parts on earth; his army lands; The town he conquers, and the tow’r commands.”

Yet more, when fair Lavinia fed the fire Before the gods, and stood beside her sire, Strange to relate, the flames, involv’d in smoke Of incense, from the sacred altar broke, Caught her dishevel’d hair and rich attire; Her crown and jewels crackled in the fire: From thence the fuming trail began to spread And lambent glories danc’d about her head. This new portent the seer with wonder views, Then pausing, thus his prophecy renews: “The nymph, who scatters flaming fires around, Shall shine with honour, shall herself be crown’d; But, caus’d by her irrevocable fate, War shall the country waste, and change the state.”

translation.original_text: <sup>126</sup>tum sperare domos defessus ibique memento <sup>127</sup>prima locare manu molirique aggere tecta. <sup>128</sup>Haec erat illa fames; haec nos suprema manebat, <sup>129</sup>exiliis positura modum. <sup>130</sup>Quare agite et primo laeti cum lumine solis <sup>131</sup>quae loca, quive habeant homines, ubi moenia gentis, <sup>132</sup>vestigemus et a portu diversa petamus. <sup>133</sup>Nunc pateras libate Iovi precibusque vocate <sup>134</sup>Anchisen genitorem, et vina reponite mensis. <sup>135</sup>Sic deinde effatus frondenti tempora ramo <sup>136</sup>implicat et geniumque loci primamque deorum <sup>137</sup>Tellurem nymphasque et adhuc ignota precatur <sup>138</sup>flumina, tum Noctem Noctisque orientia signa <sup>139</sup>Idaeumque Iovem Phrygiamque ex ordine matrem <sup>140</sup>invocat et duplicis caeloque ereboque parentis. <sup>141</sup>Hic pater omnipotens ter caelo clarus ab alto <sup>142</sup>intonuit radiisque ardentem lucis et auro <sup>143</sup>ipse manu quatiens ostendit ab aethere nubem. <sup>144</sup>Diditur hic subito Troiana per agmina rumor <sup>145</sup>advenisse diem, quo debita moenia condant. <sup>146</sup>Certatim instaurant epulas atque omine magno <sup>147</sup>crateras laeti statuunt et vina coronant. <sup>148</sup>Postera cum prima lustrabat lampade terras <sup>149</sup>orta dies, urbem et finis et litora gentis <sup>150</sup>diversi explorant: haec fontis stagna Numici,
translation.text: Latinus, frighted with this dire ostent, For counsel to his father Faunus went, And sought the shades renown’d for prophecy Which near Albunea’s sulph’rous fountain lie. To these the Latian and the Sabine land Fly, when distress’d, and thence relief demand. The priest on skins of off’rings takes his ease, And nightly visions in his slumber sees; A swarm of thin aerial shapes appears, And, flutt’ring round his temples, deafs his ears: These he consults, the future fates to know, From pow’rs above, and from the fiends below. Here, for the gods’ advice, Latinus flies, Off’ring a hundred sheep for sacrifice: Their woolly fleeces, as the rites requir’d, He laid beneath him, and to rest retir’d. No sooner were his eyes in slumber bound, When, from above, a more than mortal sound Invades his ears; and thus the vision spoke: “Seek not, my seed, in Latian bands to yoke Our fair Lavinia, nor the gods provoke. A foreign son upon thy shore descends, Whose martial fame from pole to pole extends. His race, in arms and arts of peace renown’d, Not Latium shall contain, nor Europe bound: ’Tis theirs whate’er the sun surveys around.” These answers, in the silent night receiv’d, The king himself divulg’d, the land believ’d: The fame thro’ all the neighb’ring nations flew, When now the Trojan navy was in view.

translation.original_text: <sup>151</sup>hunc Thybrim fluvium, hic fortis habitare Latinos. <sup>152</sup>Tum satus Anchisa delectos ordine ab omni <sup>153</sup>centum oratores augusta ad moenia regis <sup>154</sup>ire iubet, ramis velatos Palladis omnis, <sup>155</sup>donaque ferre viro pacemque exposcere Teucris. <sup>156</sup>Haud mora, festinant iussi rapidisque feruntur <sup>157</sup>passibus. Ipse humili designat moenia fossa <sup>158</sup>moliturque locum primasque in litore sedes <sup>159</sup>castrorum in morem pinnis atque aggere cingit. <sup>160</sup>Iamque iter emensi turris ac tecta Latinorum <sup>161</sup>ardua cernebant iuvenes muroque subibant. <sup>162</sup>Ante urbem pueri et primaevo flore iuventus <sup>163</sup>exercentur equis domitantque in pulvere currus <sup>164</sup>aut acris tendunt arcus aut lenta lacertis <sup>165</sup>spicula contorquent cursuque ictuque lacessunt: <sup>166</sup>cum praevectus equo longaevi regis ad auris <sup>167</sup>nuntius ingentis ignota in veste reportat <sup>168</sup>advenisse viros. Ille intra tecta vocari <sup>169</sup>imperat et solio medius consedit avito. <sup>170</sup>Tectum augustum ingens. centum sublime columnis, <sup>171</sup>urbe fuit summa, Laurentis regia Pici, <sup>172</sup>horrendum silvis et religione parentum. <sup>173</sup>Hic sceptra accipere et primos attollere fasces <sup>174</sup>regibus omen erat, hoc illis curia templum, <sup>175</sup>hae sacris sedes epulis, hic ariete caeso
translation.text: Beneath a shady tree, the hero spread His table on the turf, with cakes of bread; And, with his chiefs, on forest fruits he fed. They sate; and, (not without the god’s command,) Their homely fare dispatch’d, the hungry band Invade their trenchers next, and soon devour, To mend the scanty meal, their cakes of flour. Ascanius this observ’d, and smiling said: “See, we devour the plates on which we fed.” The speech had omen, that the Trojan race Should find repose, and this the time and place. Aeneas took the word, and thus replies, Confessing fate with wonder in his eyes: “All hail, O earth! all hail, my household gods! Behold the destin’d place of your abodes! For thus Anchises prophesied of old, And this our fatal place of rest foretold: ‘When, on a foreign shore, instead of meat, By famine forc’d, your trenchers you shall eat, Then ease your weary Trojans will attend, And the long labours of your voyage end. Remember on that happy coast to build, And with a trench inclose the fruitful field.’ This was that famine, this the fatal place Which ends the wand’ring of our exil’d race. Then, on tomorrow’s dawn, your care employ, To search the land, and where the cities lie, And what the men; but give this day to joy. Now pour to Jove; and, after Jove is blest, Call great Anchises to the genial feast: Crown high the goblets with a cheerful draught; Enjoy the present hour; adjourn the future thought.”

translation.original_text: <sup>176</sup>perpetuis soliti patres considere mensis. <sup>177</sup>Quin etiam veterum effigies ex ordine avorum <sup>178</sup>antiqua e cedro, Italusque paterque Sabinus <sup>179</sup>vitisator, curvam servans sub imagine falcem, <sup>180</sup>Saturnusque senex Ianique bifrontis imago <sup>181</sup>vestibulo astabant, aliique ab origine reges <sup>182</sup>Martiaque ob patriam pugnando volnera passi. <sup>183</sup>Multaque praeterea sacris in postibus arma, <sup>184</sup>captivi pendent currus curvaeque secures <sup>185</sup>et cristae capitum et portarum ingentia claustra <sup>186</sup>spiculaque clipeique ereptaque rostra carinis. <sup>187</sup>Ipse Quirinali lituo parvaque sedebat <sup>188</sup>succinctus trabea laevaque ancile gerebat <sup>189</sup>Picus, equum domitor; quem capta cupidine coniunx <sup>190</sup>aurea percussum virga versumque venenis <sup>191</sup>fecit avem Circe sparsitque coloribus alas. <sup>192</sup>Tali intus templo divom patriaque Latinus <sup>193</sup>sede sedens Teucros ad sese in tecta vocavit, <sup>194</sup>atque haec ingressis placido prior edidit ore: <sup>195</sup>Dicite, Dardanidae (neque enim nescimus et urbem <sup>196</sup>et genus, auditique advertitis aequore cursum), <sup>197</sup>quid petitis ? Quae causa rates aut cuius egentis <sup>198</sup>litus ad Ausonium tot per vada caerula vexit? <sup>199</sup>Sive errore viae seu tempestatibus acti, <sup>200</sup>qualia multa mari nautae patiuntur in alto,
translation.text:
Thus having said, the hero bound his brows With leafy branches, then perform’d his vows; Adoring first the genius of the place, Then Earth, the mother of the heav’nly race, The nymphs, and native godheads yet unknown, And Night, and all the stars that gild her sable throne, And ancient Cybel, and Idaean Jove, And last his sire below, and mother queen above. Then heav’n’s high monarch thunder’d thrice aloud, And thrice he shook aloft a golden cloud. Soon thro’ the joyful camp a rumour flew, The time was come their city to renew. Then ev’ry brow with cheerful green is crown’d, The feasts are doubled, and the bowls go round.

When next the rosy morn disclos’d the day, The scouts to sev’ral parts divide their way, To learn the natives’ names, their towns explore, The coasts and trendings of the crooked shore: Here Tiber flows, and here Numicus stands; Here warlike Latins hold the happy lands. The pious chief, who sought by peaceful ways To found his empire, and his town to raise, A hundred youths from all his train selects, And to the Latian court their course directs, (The spacious palace where their prince resides,) And all their heads with wreaths of olive hides. They go commission’d to require a peace, And carry presents to procure access. Thus while they speed their pace, the prince designs His new-elected seat, and draws the lines. The Trojans round the place a rampire cast, And palisades about the trenches plac’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>201</sup>fluminis intrastis ripas portuque sedetis, <sup>202</sup>ne fugite hospitium neve ignorate Latinos <sup>203</sup>Saturni gentem, haud vinclo nec legibus aequam, <sup>204</sup>sponte sua veterisque dei se more tenentem. <sup>205</sup>Atque equidem memini (fama est obscurior annis) <sup>206</sup>Auruncos ita ferre senes, his ortus ut agris <sup>207</sup>Dardanus Idaeas Phrygiae penetravit ad urbes <sup>208</sup>Threiciamque Samum, quae nunc Samothracia fertur. <sup>209</sup>Hinc illum, Corythi Tyrrhena ab sede profectum, <sup>210</sup>aurea nunc solio stellantis regia caeli <sup>211</sup>accipit et numerum divorum altaribus auget. <sup>212</sup>Dixerat, et dicta Ilioneus sic voce secutus: <sup>213</sup>Rex, genus egregium Fauni, nec fluctibus actos, <sup>214</sup>atra subegit hiemps vestris succedere terris <sup>215</sup>nec sidus regione viae litusve fefellit: <sup>216</sup>consilio hanc omnes animisque volentibus urbem, <sup>217</sup>adferimur, pulsi regnis, quae maxima quondam <sup>218</sup>extremo veniens Sol aspiciebat Olympo. <sup>219</sup>Ab Iove principium generis, Iove Dardana pubes <sup>220</sup>gaudet avo, rex ipse Iovis de gente suprema, <sup>221</sup>Troius Aeneas, tua nos ad limina misit. <sup>222</sup>Quanta per Idaeos saevis effusa Mycenis <sup>223</sup>tempestas ierit campos, quibus actus uterque <sup>224</sup>Europae atque Asiae fatis concurrerit orbis, <sup>225</sup>audiit et siquem tellus extrema refuso
translation.text: Meantime the train, proceeding on their way, From far the town and lofty tow’rs survey; At length approach the walls. Without the gate, They see the boys and Latian youth debate The martial prizes on the dusty plain: Some drive the cars, and some the coursers rein; Some bend the stubborn bow for victory, And some with darts their active sinews try. A posting messenger, dispatch’d from hence, Of this fair troop advis’d their aged prince, That foreign men of mighty stature came; Uncouth their habit, and unknown their name. The king ordains their entrance, and ascends His regal seat, surrounded by his friends.

translation.original_text: <sup>226</sup>summovet oceano et siquem extenta plagarum <sup>227</sup>quattuor in medio dirimit plaga solis iniqui. <sup>228</sup>Diluvio ex illo tot vasta per aequora vecti <sup>229</sup>dis sedem exiguam patriis litusque rogamus <sup>230</sup>innocuum et cunctis undamque auramque patentem. <sup>231</sup>Non erimus regno indecores, nec vestra feretur <sup>232</sup>fama levis tantique abolescet gratia facti, <sup>233</sup>nec Troiam Ausonios gremio excepisse pigebit. <sup>234</sup>Fata per Aeneae iuro dextramque potentem <sup>235</sup>sive fide seu quis bello est expertus et armis: <sup>236</sup>multi nos populi, multae (ne temne, quod ultro <sup>237</sup>praeferimus manibus vittas ac verba precantia) <sup>238</sup>et petiere sibi et voluere adiungere gentes; <sup>239</sup>sed nos fata deum vestras exquirere terras <sup>240</sup>imperiis egere suis. Hinc Dardanus ortus; <sup>241</sup>huc repetit iussisque ingentibus urguet Apollo <sup>242</sup>Tyrrhenum ad Thybrim et fontis vada sacra Numici. <sup>243</sup>Dat tibi praeterea fortunae parva prioris <sup>244</sup>munera, reliquias Troia ex ardente receptas. <sup>245</sup>Hoc pater Anchises auro libabat ad aras; <sup>246</sup>hoc Priami gestamen erat, cum iura vocatis <sup>247</sup>more daret populis, sceptrumque sacerque tiaras <sup>248</sup>Iliadumque labor vestes. <sup>249</sup>Talibus Ilionei dictis defixa Latinus <sup>250</sup>obtutu tenet ora soloque immobilis haeret
translation.text:
The palace built by Picus, vast and proud, Supported by a hundred pillars stood, And round incompass’d with a rising wood. The pile o’erlook’d the town, and drew the sight; Surpris’d at once with reverence and delight. There kings receiv’d the marks of sov’reign pow’r; In state the monarchs march’d; the lictors bore Their awful axes and the rods before. Here the tribunal stood, the house of pray’r, And here the sacred senators repair; All at large tables, in long order set, A ram their off’ring, and a ram their meat. Above the portal, carv’d in cedar wood, Plac’d in their ranks, their godlike grandsires stood; Old Saturn, with his crooked scythe, on high; And Italus, that led the colony; And ancient Janus, with his double face, And bunch of keys, the porter of the place. There good Sabinus, planter of the vines, On a short pruning hook his head reclines, And studiously surveys his gen’rous wines; Then warlike kings, who for their country fought, And honourable wounds from battle brought. Around the posts hung helmets, darts, and spears, And captive chariots, axes, shields, and bars, And broken beaks of ships, the trophies of their wars. Above the rest, as chief of all the band, Was Picus plac’d, a buckler in his hand; His other wav’d a long divining wand. Girt in his Gabin gown the hero sate, Yet could not with his art avoid his fate: For Circe long had lov’d the youth in vain, Till love, refus’d, converted to disdain: Then, mixing pow’rful herbs, with magic art, She chang’d his form, who could not change his heart; Constrain’d him in a bird, and made him fly, With party-colour’d plumes, a chatt’ring pie.

In this high temple, on a chair of state, The seat of audience, old Latinus sate; Then gave admission to the Trojan train; And thus with pleasing accents he began: “Tell me, ye Trojans, for that name you own, Nor is your course upon our coasts unknown; Say what you seek, and whither were you bound: Were you by stress of weather cast aground? Such dangers as on seas are often seen, And oft befall to miserable men, Or come, your shipping in our ports to lay, Spent and disabled in so long a way? Say what you want: the Latians you shall find Not forc’d to goodness, but by will inclin’d; For, since the time of Saturn’s holy reign, His hospitable customs we retain. I call to mind (but time the tale has worn) Th’ Arunci told, that Dardanus, tho’ born On Latian plains, yet sought the Phrygian shore, And Samothracia, Samos call’d before. From Tuscan Coritum he claim’d his birth; But after, when exempt from mortal earth, From thence ascended to his kindred skies, A god, and, as a god, augments their sacrifice.”

translation.original_text: <sup>251</sup>intentos volvens oculos. Nec purpura regem <sup>252</sup>picta movet nec sceptra movent Priameia tantum, <sup>253</sup>quantum in conubio natae thalamoque moratur, <sup>254</sup>et veteris Fauni voluit sub pectore sortem, <sup>255</sup>hunc illum fatis externa ab sede profectum <sup>256</sup>portendi generum paribusque in regna vocari <sup>257</sup>auspiciis, huic progeniem virtute futuram <sup>258</sup>egregiam et totum quae viribus occupet orbem. <sup>259</sup>tandem laetus ait: Di nostra incepta secundent <sup>260</sup>auguriumque suum; dabitur, Troiane, quod optas, <sup>261</sup>munera nec sperno. Non vobis rege Latino <sup>262</sup>divitis uber agri Troiaeve opulentia deerit. <sup>263</sup>Ipse modo Aeneas, nostri si tanta cupido est, <sup>264</sup>si iungi hospitio properat sociusque vocari, <sup>265</sup>adveniat voltus neve exhorrescat amicos: <sup>266</sup>illi pacis erit dextram tetigisse tyranni. <sup>267</sup>vos contra regi mea nunc mandata referte. <sup>268</sup>Est mihi nata, viro gentis quam iungere nostrae <sup>269</sup>non patrio ex adyto sortes, non plurima caelo <sup>270</sup>monstra sinunt: generos externis adfore ab oris, <sup>271</sup>hoc Latio restare canunt, qui sanguine nostrum <sup>272</sup>nomen in astra ferant. Hunc illum poscere fata <sup>273</sup>et reor et, siquid veri mens augurat, opto. <sup>274</sup>haec effatus equos numero pater eligit omni <sup>275</sup>(stabant ter centum nitidi in praesaepibus altis):
translation.text: He said. Ilioneus made this reply: “O king, of Faunus’ royal family! Nor wintry winds to Latium forc’d our way, Nor did the stars our wand’ring course betray. Willing we sought your shores; and, hither bound, The port, so long desir’d, at length we found; From our sweet homes and ancient realms expell’d; Great as the greatest that the sun beheld. The god began our line, who rules above; And, as our race, our king descends from Jove: And hither are we come, by his command, To crave admission in your happy land. How dire a tempest, from Mycenae pour’d, Our plains, our temples, and our town devour’d; What was the waste of war, what fierce alarms Shook Asia’s crown with European arms; Ev’n such have heard, if any such there be, Whose earth is bounded by the frozen sea; And such as, born beneath the burning sky And sultry sun, betwixt the tropics lie. From that dire deluge, thro’ the wat’ry waste, Such length of years, such various perils past, At last escap’d, to Latium we repair, To beg what you without your want may spare: The common water, and the common air; Sheds which ourselves will build, and mean abodes, Fit to receive and serve our banish’d gods. Nor our admission shall your realm disgrace, Nor length of time our gratitude efface. Besides, what endless honour you shall gain, To save and shelter Troy’s unhappy train! Now, by my sov’reign, and his fate, I swear, Renown’d for faith in peace, for force in war; Oft our alliance other lands desir’d, And, what we seek of you, of us requir’d. Despite not then, that in our hands we bear These holy boughs, and sue with words of pray’r. Fate and the gods, by their supreme command, Have doom’d our ships to seek the Latian land. To these abodes our fleet Apollo sends; Here Dardanus was born, and hither tends; Where Tuscan Tiber rolls with rapid force, And where Numicus opes his holy source. Besides, our prince presents, with his request, Some small remains of what his sire possess’d. This golden charger, snatch’d from burning Troy, Anchises did in sacrifice employ; This royal robe and this tiara wore Old Priam, and this golden scepter bore In full assemblies, and in solemn games; These purple vests were weav’d by Dardan dames.”

translation.original_text: <sup>276</sup>omnibus extemplo Teucris iubet ordine duci <sup>277</sup>instratos ostro alipedes pictisque tapetis; <sup>278</sup>aurea pectoribus demissa monilia pendent, <sup>279</sup>tecti auro fulvum mandunt sub dentibus aurum; <sup>280</sup>absenti Aeneae currum geminosque iugalis <sup>281</sup>semine ab aetherio, spirantis naribus ignem, <sup>282</sup>illorum de gente, patri quos daedala Circe <sup>283</sup>supposita de matre nothos furata creavit. <sup>284</sup>Talibus Aeneadae donis dictisque Latini <sup>285</sup>sublimes in equis redeunt pacemque reportant. <sup>286</sup>Ecce autem Inachiis sese referebat ab Argis <sup>287</sup>saeva Iovis coniunx aurasque invecta tenebat, <sup>288</sup>et laetum Aenean classemque ex aethere longe <sup>289</sup>Dardaniam Siculo prospexit ab usque Pachyno. <sup>290</sup>moliri iam tecta videt, iam fidere terrae, <sup>291</sup>deseruisse rates: stetit acri fixa dolore. <sup>292</sup>Tum quassans caput haec effundit pectore dicta: <sup>293</sup>Heu stirpem invisam et fatis contraria nostris <sup>294</sup>fata Phrygum ! Num Sigeis occumbere campis, <sup>295</sup>num capti potuere capi, num incensa cremavit <sup>296</sup>Troia viros ? Medias acies mediosque per ignis <sup>297</sup>invenere viam. At, credo, mea numina tandem <sup>298</sup>fessa iacent odiis aut exsaturata quievi. <sup>299</sup>Quin etiam patria excussos infesta per undas <sup>300</sup>ausa sequi et profugis toto me opponere ponto !
translation.text: Thus while he spoke, Latinus roll’d around His eyes, and fix’d a while upon the ground. Intent he seem’d, and anxious in his breast; Not by the scepter mov’d, or kingly vest, But pond’ring future things of wondrous weight; Succession, empire, and his daughter’s fate. On these he mus’d within his thoughtful mind, And then revolv’d what Faunus had divin’d. This was the foreign prince, by fate decreed To share his scepter, and Lavinia’s bed; This was the race that sure portents foreshew To sway the world, and land and sea subdue. At length he rais’d his cheerful head, and spoke: “The pow’rs,” said he, “the pow’rs we both invoke, To you, and yours, and mine, propitious be, And firm our purpose with their augury! Have what you ask; your presents I receive; Land, where and when you please, with ample leave; Partake and use my kingdom as your own; All shall be yours, while I command the crown: And, if my wish’d alliance please your king, Tell him he should not send the peace, but bring. Then let him not a friend’s embraces fear; The peace is made when I behold him here. Besides this answer, tell my royal guest, I add to his commands my own request: One only daughter heirs my crown and state, Whom not our oracles, nor Heav’n, nor fate, Nor frequent prodigies, permit to join With any native of th’ Ausonian line. A foreign son-in-law shall come from far (Such is our doom), a chief renown’d in war, Whose race shall bear aloft the Latian name, And thro’ the conquer’d world diffuse our fame. Himself to be the man the fates require, I firmly judge, and, what I judge, desire.”

translation.original_text: <sup>301</sup>Absumptae in Teucros vires caelique marisque. <sup>302</sup>Quid Syrtes aut Scylla mihi, quid vasta Charybdis <sup>303</sup>profuit? Optato conduntur Thybridis alveo, <sup>304</sup>securi pelagi atque mei. Mars perdere gentem <sup>305</sup>immanem Lapithum valuit, concessit in iras <sup>306</sup>ipse deum antiquam genitor Calydona Dianae, <sup>307</sup>quod scelus aut Lapithis tantum aut Calydona merentem? <sup>308</sup>Ast ego magna Iovis coniunx, nil linquere inausum <sup>309</sup>quae potui infelix, quae memet in omnia verti, <sup>310</sup>vincor ab Aenea. Quod si mea numina non sunt <sup>311</sup>magna satis, dubitem haud equidem implorare quod usquam est: <sup>312</sup>flectere si nequeo superos, Acheronta movebo. <sup>313</sup>Non dabitur regnis, esto, prohibere Latinis, <sup>314</sup>atque immota manet fatis Lavinia coniunx: <sup>315</sup>at trahere atque moras tantis licet addere rebus, <sup>316</sup>at licet amborum populos exscindere regum. <sup>317</sup>Hac gener atque socer coeant mercede suorum: <sup>318</sup>sanguine Troiano et Rutulo dotabere, virgo, <sup>319</sup>et Bellona manet te pronuba. Nec face tantum <sup>320</sup>Cisseis praegnans ignis enixa iugalis <sup>321</sup>quin idem Veneri partus suus et Paris alter <sup>322</sup>funestaeque iterum recidiva in Pergama taedae. <sup>323</sup>Haec ubi dicta dedit, terras Horrenda petivit: <sup>324</sup>luctificam Allecto dirarum ab sede dearum <sup>325</sup>infernisque ciet tenebris, cui tristia bella
translation.text:
He said, and then on each bestow’d a steed. Three hundred horses, in high stables fed, Stood ready, shining all, and smoothly dress’d: Of these he chose the fairest and the best, To mount the Trojan troop. At his command The steeds caparison’d with purple stand, With golden trappings, glorious to behold, And champ betwixt their teeth the foaming gold. Then to his absent guest the king decreed A pair of coursers born of heav’nly breed, Who from their nostrils breath’d ethereal fire; Whom Circe stole from her celestial sire, By substituting mares produc’d on earth, Whose wombs conceiv’d a more than mortal birth. These draw the chariot which Latinus sends, And the rich present to the prince commends. Sublime on stately steeds the Trojans borne, To their expecting lord with peace return.

But jealous Juno, from Pachynus’ height, As she from Argos took her airy flight, Beheld with envious eyes this hateful sight. She saw the Trojan and his joyful train Descend upon the shore, desert the main, Design a town, and, with unhop’d success, Th’ embassadors return with promis’d peace. Then, pierc’d with pain, she shook her haughty head, Sigh’d from her inward soul, and thus she said: “O hated offspring of my Phrygian foes! O fates of Troy, which Juno’s fates oppose! Could they not fall unpitied on the plain, But slain revive, and, taken, scape again? When execrable Troy in ashes lay, Thro’ fires and swords and seas they forc’d their way. Then vanquish’d Juno must in vain contend, Her rage disarm’d, her empire at an end. Breathless and tir’d, is all my fury spent? Or does my glutted spleen at length relent? As if ’twere little from their town to chase, I thro’ the seas pursued their exil’d race; Ingag’d the heav’ns, oppos’d the stormy main; But billows roar’d, and tempests rag’d in vain. What have my Scyllas and my Syrtes done, When these they overpass, and those they shun? On Tiber’s shores they land, secure of fate, Triumphant o’er the storms and Juno’s hate. Mars could in mutual blood the Centaurs bathe, And Jove himself gave way to Cynthia’s wrath, Who sent the tusky boar to Calydon; What great offence had either people done? But I, the consort of the Thunderer, Have wag’d a long and unsuccessful war, With various arts and arms in vain have toil’d, And by a mortal man at length am foil’d. If native pow’r prevail not, shall I doubt To seek for needful succour from without? If Jove and Heav’n my just desires deny, Hell shall the pow’r of Heav’n and Jove supply. Grant that the Fates have firm’d, by their decree, The Trojan race to reign in Italy; At least I can defer the nuptial day, And with protracted wars the peace delay: With blood the dear alliance shall be bought, And both the people near destruction brought; So shall the son-in-law and father join, With ruin, war, and waste of either line. O fatal maid, thy marriage is endow’d With Phrygian, Latian, and Rutulian blood! Bellona leads thee to thy lover’s hand; Another queen brings forth another brand, To burn with foreign fires another land! A second Paris, diff’ring but in name, Shall fire his country with a second flame.”

translation.original_text: <sup>326</sup>iraeque insidiaeque et crimina noxia cordi. <sup>327</sup>Odit et ipse pater Pluton, odere sorores <sup>328</sup>Tartareae monstrum: tot sese vertit in ora, <sup>329</sup>tam saevae facies, tot pullulat atra colubris. <sup>330</sup>Quam Iuno his acuit verbis ac talia fatur: <sup>331</sup>Hunc mihi da proprium, virgo sata Nocte, laborem, <sup>332</sup>hanc operam, ne noster honos infractave cedat <sup>333</sup>fama loco, neu conubiis ambire Latinum <sup>334</sup>Aeneadae possint Italosve obsidere finis. <sup>335</sup>Tu potes unanimos armare in proelia fratres <sup>336</sup>atque odiis versare domos, tu verbera tectis <sup>337</sup>funereasque inferre faces, tibi nomina mille, <sup>338</sup>mille nocendi artes. Fecundum concute pectus, <sup>339</sup>disice compositam pacem, sere crimina belli: <sup>340</sup>arma velit poscatque simul rapiatque inventus. <sup>341</sup>Exin Gorgoneis Allecto infecta venenis <sup>342</sup>principio Latium et Laurentis tecta tyranni <sup>343</sup>celsa petit tacitumque obsedit limen Amatae, <sup>344</sup>quam super adventu Teucrum Turnique hymenaeis <sup>345</sup>femineae ardentem curaeque iraeque coquebant. <sup>346</sup>Huic dea caeruleis unum de crinibus anguem <sup>347</sup>conicit inque sinum praecordia ad intuma subdit, <sup>348</sup>quo furibunda domum monstro permisceat omnem. <sup>349</sup>Ille inter vestes et levia pectora lapsus <sup>350</sup>volvitur attactu nullo fallitque furentem,
translation.text: Thus having said, she sinks beneath the ground, With furious haste, and shoots the Stygian sound, To rouse Alecto from th’ infernal seat Of her dire sisters, and their dark retreat. This Fury, fit for her intent, she chose; One who delights in wars and human woes. Ev’n Pluto hates his own misshapen race; Her sister Furies fly her hideous face; So frightful are the forms the monster takes, So fierce the hissings of her speckled snakes. Her Juno finds, and thus inflames her spite: “O virgin daughter of eternal Night, Give me this once thy labour, to sustain My right, and execute my just disdain. Let not the Trojans, with a feign’d pretence Of proffer’d peace, delude the Latian prince. Expel from Italy that odious name, And let not Juno suffer in her fame. ’Tis thine to ruin realms, o’erturn a state, Betwixt the dearest friends to raise debate, And kindle kindred blood to mutual hate. Thy hand o’er towns the fun’ral torch displays, And forms a thousand ills ten thousand ways. Now shake, out thy fruitful breast, the seeds Of envy, discord, and of cruel deeds: Confound the peace establish’d, and prepare Their souls to hatred, and their hands to war.”

translation.original_text: <sup>351</sup>vipeream inspirans animam: fit tortile collo <sup>352</sup>aurum ingens coluber, fit longae taenia vittae <sup>353</sup>innectitque comas, et membris lubricus errat. <sup>354</sup>Ac dum prima lues udo sublapsa veneno <sup>355</sup>pertemptat sensus atque ossibus implicat ignem <sup>356</sup>necdum animus toto percepit pectore flammam, <sup>357</sup>mollius et solito matrum de more locuta est, <sup>358</sup>multa super nata lacrimans Phrygiisque hymenaeis: <sup>359</sup>Exsulibusne datur ducenda Lavinia Teucris, <sup>360</sup>O genitor, nec te miseret gnataeque tuique ? <sup>361</sup>Nec matris miseret, quam primo aquilone relinquet <sup>362</sup>perfidus alta petens abducta virgine praedo? <sup>363</sup>An non sic Phrygius penetrat Lacedaemona pastor <sup>364</sup>Ledaeamque Helenam Troianas vexit ad urbes ? <sup>365</sup>Quid tua sancta fides, quid cura antiqua tuorum <sup>366</sup>et consanguineo totiens data dextera Turno? <sup>367</sup>Si gener externa petitur de gente Latinis <sup>368</sup>idque sedet Faunique premunt te iussa parentis, <sup>369</sup>omnem equidem sceptris terram quae libera nostris <sup>370</sup>dissidet, externam reor et sic dicere divos. <sup>371</sup>Et Turno, si prima domus repetatur origo, <sup>372</sup>Inachus Acrisiusque patres mediaeque Mycenae. <sup>373</sup>His ubi nequiquam dictis experta Latinum <sup>374</sup>contra stare videt penitusque in viscera lapsum <sup>375</sup>serpentis furiale malum totamque pererrat,
translation.text:
Smear’d as she was with black Gorgonian blood, The Fury sprang above the Stygian flood; And on her wicker wings, sublime thro’ night, She to the Latian palace took her flight: There sought the queen’s apartment, stood before The peaceful threshold, and besieg’d the door. Restless Amata lay, her swelling breast Fir’d with disdain for Turnus dispossess’d, And the new nuptials of the Trojan guest. From her black bloody locks the Fury shakes Her darling plague, the fav’rite of her snakes; With her full force she threw the poisonous dart, And fix’d it deep within Amata’s heart, That, thus envenom’d, she might kindle rage, And sacrifice to strife her house and husband’s age. Unseen, unfelt, the fiery serpent skims Betwixt her linen and her naked limbs; His baleful breath inspiring, as he glides, Now like a chain around her neck he rides, Now like a fillet to her head repairs, And with his circling volumes folds her hairs. At first the silent venom slid with ease, And seiz’d her cooler senses by degrees; Then, ere th’ infected mass was fir’d too far, In plaintive accents she began the war, And thus bespoke her husband: “Shall,” she said, “A wand’ring prince enjoy Lavinia’s bed? If nature plead not in a parent’s heart, Pity my tears, and pity her desert. I know, my dearest lord, the time will come, You’d in vain, reverse your cruel doom; The faithless pirate soon will set to sea, And bear the royal virgin far away! A guest like him, a Trojan guest before, In shew of friendship sought the Spartan shore, And ravish’d Helen from her husband bore. Think on a king’s inviolable word; And think on Turnus, her once plighted lord: To this false foreigner you give your throne, And wrong a friend, a kinsman, and a son. Resume your ancient care; and, if the god Your sire, and you, resolve on foreign blood, Know all are foreign, in a larger sense, Not born your subjects, or deriv’d from hence. Then, if the line of Turnus you retrace, He springs from Inachus of Argive race.”

But when she saw her reasons idly spent, And could not move him from his fix’d intent, She flew to rage; for now the snake possess’d Her vital parts, and poison’d all her breast; She raves, she runs with a distracted pace, And fills with horrid howls the public place. And, as young striplings whip the top for sport, On the smooth pavement of an empty court; The wooden engine flies and whirls about, Admir’d, with clamours, of the beardless rout; They lash aloud; each other they provoke, And lend their little souls at ev’ry stroke: Thus fares the queen; and thus her fury blows Amidst the crowd, and kindles as she goes. Nor yet content, she strains her malice more, And adds new ills to those contriv’d before: She flies the town, and, mixing with a throng Of madding matrons, bears the bride along, Wand’ring thro’ woods and wilds, and devious ways, And with these arts the Trojan match delays. She feign’d the rites of Bacchus; cried aloud, And to the buxom god the virgin vow’d. “Evoe! O Bacchus!” thus began the song; And “Evoe!” answer’d all the female throng. “O virgin! worthy thee alone!” she cried; “O worthy thee alone!” the crew replied. “For thee she feeds her hair, she leads thy dance, And with thy winding ivy wreathes her lance.” Like fury seiz’d the rest; the progress known, All seek the mountains, and forsake the town: All, clad in skins of beasts, the jav’lin bear, Give to the wanton winds their flowing hair, And shrieks and shoutings rend the suff’ring air. The queen herself, inspir’d with rage divine, Shook high above her head a flaming pine; Then roll’d her haggard eyes around the throng, And sung, in Turnus’ name, the nuptial song: “Io, ye Latian dames! if any here Hold your unhappy queen, Amata, dear; If there be here,” she said, “who dare maintain My right, nor think the name of mother vain; Unbind your fillets, loose your flowing hair, And orgies and nocturnal rites prepare.”

translation.original_text: <sup>376</sup>tum vero infelix, ingentibus excita monstris, <sup>377</sup>immensam sine more furit lymphata per urbem. <sup>378</sup>Ceu quondam torto volitans sub verbere turbo, <sup>379</sup>quem pueri magno in gyro vacua atria circum <sup>380</sup>intenti ludo exercent; ille actus habena <sup>381</sup>curvatis fertur spatiis; stupet inscia supra <sup>382</sup>inpubesque manus, mirata volubile buxum; <sup>383</sup>dant animos plagae: non cursu segnior illo <sup>384</sup>per medias urbes agitur populosque feroces. <sup>385</sup>Quin etiam in silvas, simulato numine Bacchi, <sup>386</sup>maius adorta nefas maioremque orsa furorem <sup>387</sup>evolat et natam frondosis montibus abdit, <sup>388</sup>quo thalamum eripiat Teucris taedasque moretur, <sup>389</sup>Euhoe Bacche, fremens, solum te virgine dignum <sup>390</sup>vociferans, etenim mollis tibi sumere thyrsos, <sup>391</sup>te lustrare choro, sacrum tibi pascere crinem. <sup>392</sup>Fama volat, furiisque accensas pectore matres <sup>393</sup>idem omnis simul ardor agit nova quaerere tecta: <sup>394</sup>deseruere domos, ventis dant colla comasque, <sup>395</sup>ast aliae tremulis ululatibus aethera complent, <sup>396</sup>pampineasque gerunt incinctae pellibus hastas; <sup>397</sup>ipsa inter medias flagrantem fervida pinum <sup>398</sup>sustinet ac natae Turnique canit hymenaeos, <sup>399</sup>sanguineam torquens aciem, torvumque repente <sup>400</sup>clamat: Io matres, audite, ubi quaeque, Latinae:
translation.text: Amata’s breast the Fury thus invades, And fires with rage, amid the sylvan shades; Then, when she found her venom spread so far, The royal house embroil’d in civil war, Rais’d on her dusky wings, she cleaves the skies, And seeks the palace where young Turnus lies. His town, as fame reports, was built of old By Danae, pregnant with almighty gold, Who fled her father’s rage, and, with a train Of following Argives, thro’ the stormy main, Driv’n by the southern blasts, was fated here to reign. ’Twas Ardua once; now Ardea’s name it bears; Once a fair city, now consum’d with years. Here, in his lofty palace, Turnus lay, Betwixt the confines of the night and day, Secure in sleep. The Fury laid aside Her looks and limbs, and with new methods tried The foulness of th’ infernal form to hide. Propp’d on a staff, she takes a trembling mien: Her face is furrow’d, and her front obscene; Deep-dinted wrinkles on her cheek she draws; Sunk are her eyes, and toothless are her jaws; Her hoary hair with holy fillets bound, Her temples with an olive wreath are crown’d. Old Chalybe, who kept the sacred fane Of Juno, now she seem’d, and thus began, Appearing in a dream, to rouse the careless man: “Shall Turnus then such endless toil sustain In fighting fields, and conquer towns in vain? Win, for a Trojan head to wear the prize, Usurp thy crown, enjoy thy victories? The bride and scepter which thy blood has bought, The king transfers; and foreign heirs are sought. Go now, deluded man, and seek again New toils, new dangers, on the dusty plain. Repel the Tuscan foes; their city seize; Protect the Latians in luxurious ease. This dream all-pow’rful Juno sends; I bear Her mighty mandates, and her words you hear. Haste; arm your Ardeans; issue to the plain; With fate to friend, assault the Trojan train: Their thoughtless chiefs, their painted ships, that lie In Tiber’s mouth, with fire and sword destroy. The Latian king, unless he shall submit, Own his old promise, and his new forget; Let him, in arms, the pow’r of Turnus prove, And learn to fear whom he disdains to love. For such is Heav’n’s command.” The youthful prince With scorn replied, and made this bold defence: “You tell me, mother, what I knew before: The Phrygian fleet is landed on the shore. I neither fear nor will provoke the war; My fate is Juno’s most peculiar care. But time has made you dote, and vainly tell Of arms imagin’d in your lonely cell. Go; be the temple and the gods your care; Permit to men the thought of peace and war.”

translation.original_text: <sup>401</sup>Siqua piis animis manet infelicis Amatae <sup>402</sup>gratia, si iuris materni cura remordet, <sup>403</sup>solvite crinalis vittas, capite orgia mecum. <sup>404</sup>Talem inter silvas, inter deserta ferarum, <sup>405</sup>reginam Allecto stimulis agit undique Bacchi. <sup>406</sup>Postquam visa satis primos acuisse furores <sup>407</sup>consiliumque omnemque domum vertisse Latini, <sup>408</sup>protinus hinc fuscis tristis dea tollitur alis <sup>409</sup>audacis Rutuli ad muros, quam dicitur urbem <sup>410</sup>Acrisioneis Danae fundasse colonis, <sup>411</sup>praecipiti delata noto. Locus Ardea quondam <sup>412</sup>dictus avis, et nunc magnum manet Ardea nomen, <sup>413</sup>sed fortuna fuit; tectis hic Turnus in altis <sup>414</sup>iam mediam nigra carpebat nocte quietem. <sup>415</sup>Allecto torvam faciem et furialia membra <sup>416</sup>exuit, in vultus sese transformat anilis; <sup>417</sup>et frontem obscenam rugis arat, induit albos <sup>418</sup>cum vitta crinis, tum ramum innectit olivae; <sup>419</sup>fit Calybe Iunonis anus templique sacerdos <sup>420</sup>et iuveni ante oculos his se cum vocibus offert: <sup>421</sup>Turne, tot incassum fusos patiere labores <sup>422</sup>et tua Dardaniis transcribi sceptra colonis? <sup>423</sup>Rex tibi coniugium et quaesitas sanguine dotes <sup>424</sup>abnegat, externusque in regnum quaeritur heres. <sup>425</sup>I nunc, ingratis offer te, inrise, periclis;
translation.text: These haughty words Alecto’s rage provoke, And frighted Turnus trembled as she spoke. Her eyes grow stiffen’d, and with sulphur burn; Her hideous looks and hellish form return; Her curling snakes with hissings fill the place, And open all the furies of her face: Then, darting fire from her malignant eyes, She cast him backward as he strove to rise, And, ling’ring, sought to frame some new replies. High on her head she rears two twisted snakes, Her chains she rattles, and her whip she shakes; And, churning bloody foam, thus loudly speaks: “Behold whom time has made to dote, and tell Of arms imagin’d in her lonely cell! Behold the Fates’ infernal minister! War, death, destruction, in my hand I bear.”

translation.original_text: <sup>426</sup>Tyrrhenas, i, sterne acies; tege pace Latinos. <sup>427</sup>Haec adeo tibi me, placida cum nocte iaceres, <sup>428</sup>ipsa palam fari omnipotens Saturnia iussit. <sup>429</sup>Quare age et armari pubem portisque moveri <sup>430</sup>laetus in arma para, et Phrygios qui flumine pulchro <sup>431</sup>consedere duces pictasque exure carinas. <sup>432</sup>Caelestum vis magna iubet.  Rex ipse Latinus, <sup>433</sup>ni dare coniugium et dicto parere fatetur, <sup>434</sup>sentiat et tandem Turnum experiatur in armis. <sup>435</sup>Hic iuvenis vatem inridens sic orsa vicissim <sup>436</sup>ore refert: Classis invectas Thybridis undam <sup>437</sup>non, ut rere, meas effugit nuntius auris. <sup>438</sup>Ne tantos mihi finge metus; nec regia Iuno <sup>439</sup>inmemor est nostri. <sup>440</sup>Sed te victa situ verique effeta senectus, <sup>441</sup>o mater, curis nequiquam exercet et arma <sup>442</sup>regum inter falsa vatem formidine ludit. <sup>443</sup>Cura tibi divom effigies et templa tueri: <sup>444</sup>bella viri pacemque gerent, quis bella gerenda. <sup>445</sup>Talibus Allecto dictis exarsit in iras, <sup>446</sup>at iuveni oranti subitus tremor occupat artus, <sup>447</sup>deriguere oculi: tot Erinys sibilat hydris <sup>448</sup>tantaque se facies aperit; tum flammea torquens <sup>449</sup>lumina cunctantem et quaerentem dicere plura <sup>450</sup>reppulit et geminos erexit crinibus anguis
translation.text:
Thus having said, her smould’ring torch, impress’d With her full force, she plung’d into his breast. Aghast he wak’d; and, starting from his bed, Cold sweat, in clammy drops, his limbs o’erspread. “Arms! arms!” he cries: “my sword and shield prepare!” He breathes defiance, blood, and mortal war. So, when with crackling flames a caldron fries, The bubbling waters from the bottom rise: Above the brims they force their fiery way; Black vapours climb aloft, and cloud the day.

The peace polluted thus, a chosen band He first commissions to the Latian land, In threat’ning embassy; then rais’d the rest, To meet in arms th’ intruding Trojan guest, To force the foes from the Lavinian shore, And Italy’s indanger’d peace restore. Himself alone an equal match he boasts, To fight the Phrygian and Ausonian hosts. The gods invok’d, the Rutuli prepare Their arms, and warn each other to the war. His beauty these, and those his blooming age, The rest his house and his own fame engage.

translation.original_text: <sup>451</sup>verberaque insonuit rabidoque haec addidit ore: <sup>452</sup>En ego victa situ, quam veri effeta senectus <sup>453</sup>arma inter regum falsa formidine ludit. <sup>454</sup>Respice ad haec: adsum dirarum ab sede sororum, <sup>455</sup>bella manu letumque gero. <sup>456</sup>Sic effata facem iuveni coniecit et atro <sup>457</sup>lumine fumantis fixit sub pectore taedas. <sup>458</sup>Olli somnum ingens rumpit pavor, ossaque et artus <sup>459</sup>perfundit toto proruptus corpore sudor; <sup>460</sup>arma amens fremit, arma toro tectisque requirit; <sup>461</sup>saevit amor ferri et scelerata insania belli, <sup>462</sup>ira super: magno veluti cum flamma sonore <sup>463</sup>virgea suggeritur costis undantis aëni <sup>464</sup>exsultantque aestu latices, furit intus aquaï <sup>465</sup>fumidus atque alte spumis exuberat amnis, <sup>466</sup>nec iam se capit unda, volat vapor ater ad auras. <sup>467</sup>Ergo iter ad regem polluta pace Latinum <sup>468</sup>indicit primis iuvenum et iubet arma parari, <sup>469</sup>tutari Italiam, detrudere finibus hostem: <sup>470</sup>se satis ambobus Teucrisque venire Latinisque. <sup>471</sup>Haec ubi dicta dedit divosque in vota vocavit, <sup>472</sup>certatim sese Rutuli exhortantur in arma: <sup>473</sup>hunc decus egregium formae movet atque iuventae, <sup>474</sup>hunc atavi reges, hunc claris dextera factis. <sup>475</sup>Dum Turnus Rutulos animis audacibus implet,
translation.text: While Turnus urges thus his enterprise, The Stygian Fury to the Trojans flies; New frauds invents, and takes a steepy stand, Which overlooks the vale with wide command; Where fair Ascanius and his youthful train, With horns and hounds, a hunting match ordain, And pitch their toils around the shady plain. The Fury fires the pack; they snuff, they vent, And feed their hungry nostrils with the scent. ’Twas of a well-grown stag, whose antlers rise High o’er his front; his beams invade the skies. From this light cause th’ infernal maid prepares The country churls to mischief, hate, and wars.

translation.original_text: <sup>476</sup>Allecto in Teucros Stygiis se concitat alis, <sup>477</sup>arte nova speculata locum, quo litore pulcher <sup>478</sup>insidiis cursuque feras agitabat Iulus. <sup>479</sup>Hic subitam canibus rabiem Cocytia virgo <sup>480</sup>obicit et noto naris contingit odore, <sup>481</sup>ut cervum ardentes agerent; quae prima laborum <sup>482</sup>causa fuit belloque animos accendit agrestis. <sup>483</sup>Cervus erat forma praestanti et cornibus ingens, <sup>484</sup>Tyrrhidae pueri quem matris ab ubere raptum <sup>485</sup>nutribant Tyrrhusque pater, cui regia parent <sup>486</sup>armenta et late custodia credita campi. <sup>487</sup>Adsuetum imperiis soror omni Silvia cura <sup>488</sup>mollibus intexens ornabat cornua sertis <sup>489</sup>pectebatque ferum puroque in fonte lavabat. <sup>490</sup>Ille, manum patiens mensaeque adsuetus erili, <sup>491</sup>errabat silvis rursusque ad limina nota <sup>492</sup>ipse domum sera quamvis se nocte ferebat. <sup>493</sup>Hunc procul errantem rabidae venantis Iuli <sup>494</sup>commovere canes, fluvio cum forte secundo <sup>495</sup>deflueret ripaque aestus viridante levaret. <sup>496</sup>Ipse etiam, eximiae laudis succensus amore, <sup>497</sup>Ascanius curvo direxit spicula cornu; <sup>498</sup>nec dextrae erranti deus afuit, actaque multo <sup>499</sup>perque uterum sonitu perque ilia venit harundo. <sup>500</sup>Saucius at quadrupes nota intra tecta refugit
translation.text:
The stately beast the two Tyrrhidae bred, Snatch’d from his dams, and the tame youngling fed. Their father Tyrrheus did his fodder bring, Tyrrheus, chief ranger to the Latian king: Their sister Silvia cherish’d with her care The little wanton, and did wreaths prepare To hang his budding horns, with ribbons tied His tender neck, and comb’d his silken hide, And bathed his body. Patient of command In time he grew, and, growing us’d to hand, He waited at his master’s board for food; Then sought his salvage kindred in the wood, Where grazing all the day, at night he came To his known lodgings, and his country dame.

This household beast, that us’d the woodland grounds, Was view’d at first by the young hero’s hounds, As down the stream he swam, to seek retreat In the cool waters, and to quench his heat. Ascanius young, and eager of his game, Soon bent his bow, uncertain in his aim; But the dire fiend the fatal arrow guides, Which pierc’d his bowels thro’ his panting sides. The bleeding creature issues from the floods, Possess’d with fear, and seeks his known abodes, His old familiar hearth and household gods. He falls; he fills the house with heavy groans, Implores their pity, and his pain bemoans. Young Silvia beats her breast, and cries aloud For succour from the clownish neighbourhood: The churls assemble; for the fiend, who lay In the close woody covert, urg’d their way. One with a brand yet burning from the flame, Arm’d with a knotty club another came: Whate’er they catch or find, without their care, Their fury makes an instrument of war. Tyrrheus, the foster father of the beast, Then clench’d a hatchet in his horny fist, But held his hand from the descending stroke, And left his wedge within the cloven oak, To whet their courage and their rage provoke. And now the goddess, exercis’d in ill, Who watch’d an hour to work her impious will, Ascends the roof, and to her crooked horn, Such as was then by Latian shepherds borne, Adds all her breath: the rocks and woods around, And mountains, tremble at th’ infernal sound. The sacred lake of Trivia from afar, The Veline fountains, and sulphureous Nar, Shake at the baleful blast, the signal of the war. Young mothers wildly stare, with fear possess’d, And strain their helpless infants to their breast.

translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>successitque gemens stabulis questuque cruentus <sup>502</sup>atque imploranti similis tectum omne replebat. <sup>503</sup>Silvia prima soror, palmis percussa lacertos, <sup>504</sup>auxilium vocat et duros conclamat agrestis. <sup>505</sup>Olli, pestis enim tacitis latet aspera silvis, <sup>506</sup>inprovisi adsunt, hic torre armatus obusto, <sup>507</sup>stipitis hic gravidi nodis: quod cuique repertum <sup>508</sup>rimanti, telum ira facit. Vocat agmina Tyrrhus, <sup>509</sup>quadrifidam quercum cuneis ut forte coactis <sup>510</sup>scindebat, rapta spirans immane securi. <sup>511</sup>At saeva e speculis tempus dea nacta nocendi <sup>512</sup>ardua tecta petit stabuli et de culmine summo <sup>513</sup>pastorale canit signum cornuque recurvo <sup>514</sup>Tartaream intendit vocem, qua protinus omne <sup>515</sup>contremuit nemus et silvae insonuere profundae; <sup>516</sup>audiit et Triviae longe lacus, audiit amnis <sup>517</sup>sulfurea Nar albus aqua fontesque Velini, <sup>518</sup>et trepidae matres pressere ad pectora natos. <sup>519</sup>Tum vero ad vocem celeres, qua bucina signum <sup>520</sup>dira dedit, raptis concurrunt undique telis <sup>521</sup>indomiti agricolae; nec non et Troïa pubes <sup>522</sup>Ascanio auxilium castris effundit apertis. <sup>523</sup>Direxere acies. Non iam certamine agresti, <sup>524</sup>stipitibus duris agitur sudibusve praeustis, <sup>525</sup>sed ferro ancipiti decernunt atraque late
translation.text: The clowns, a boist’rous, rude, ungovern’d crew, With furious haste to the loud summons flew. The pow’rs of Troy, then issuing on the plain, With fresh recruits their youthful chief sustain: Not theirs a raw and unexperienc’d train, But a firm body of embattled men. At first, while fortune favour’d neither side, The fight with clubs and burning brands was tried; But now, both parties reinforc’d, the fields Are bright with flaming swords and brazen shields. A shining harvest either host displays, And shoots against the sun with equal rays. Thus, when a black-brow’d gust begins to rise, White foam at first on the curl’d ocean fries; Then roars the main, the billows mount the skies; Till, by the fury of the storm full blown, The muddy bottom o’er the clouds is thrown. First Almon falls, old Tyrrheus’ eldest care, Pierc’d with an arrow from the distant war: Fix’d in his throat the flying weapon stood, And stopp’d his breath, and drank his vital blood Huge heaps of slain around the body rise: Among the rest, the rich Galesus lies; A good old man, while peace he preach’d in vain, Amidst the madness of th’ unruly train: Five herds, five bleating flocks, his pastures fill’d; His lands a hundred yoke of oxen till’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>horrescit strictis seges ensibus aeraque fulgent <sup>527</sup>sole lacessita et lucem sub nubila iactant: <sup>528</sup>fluctus uti primo coepit cum albescere vento, <sup>529</sup>paulatim sese tollit mare et altius undas <sup>530</sup>erigit, inde imo consurgit ad aethera fundo. <sup>531</sup>Hic iuvenis primam ante aciem stridente sagitta, <sup>532</sup>natorum Tyrrhi fuerat qui maximus, Almo, <sup>533</sup>sternitur; haesit enim sub gutture volnus et udae <sup>534</sup>vocis iter tenuemque inclusit sanguine vitam. <sup>535</sup>Corpora multa virum circa seniorque Galaesus, <sup>536</sup>dum paci medium se offert, iustissimus unus <sup>537</sup>qui fuit Ausoniisque olim ditissimus arvis: <sup>538</sup>quinque greges illi balantum, quina redibant <sup>539</sup>armenta, et terram centum vertebat aratris. <sup>540</sup>Atque ea per campos aequo dum Marte geruntur, <sup>541</sup>promissi dea facta potens, ubi sanguine bellum <sup>542</sup>imbuit et primae commisit funera pugnae, <sup>543</sup>deserit Hesperiam et caeli convexa per auras <sup>544</sup>Iunonem victrix adfatur voce superba: <sup>545</sup>En, perfecta tibi bello discordia tristi: <sup>546</sup>dic in amicitiam coeant et foedera iungant. <sup>547</sup>Quando quidem Ausonio respersi sanguine Teucros, <sup>548</sup>hoc etiam his addam, tua si mihi certa voluntas: <sup>549</sup>finitimas in bella feram rumoribus urbes <sup>550</sup>accendamque animos insani Martis amore,
translation.text: Thus, while in equal scales their fortune stood The Fury bath’d them in each other’s blood; Then, having fix’d the fight, exulting flies, And bears fulfill’d her promise to the skies. To Juno thus she speaks: “Behold! It is done, The blood already drawn, the war begun; The discord is complete; nor can they cease The dire debate, nor you command the peace. Now, since the Latian and the Trojan brood Have tasted vengeance and the sweets of blood; Speak, and my pow’r shall add this office more: The neighbr’ing nations of th’ Ausonian shore Shall hear the dreadful rumour, from afar, Of arm’d invasion, and embrace the war.” Then Juno thus: “The grateful work is done, The seeds of discord sow’d, the war begun; Frauds, fears, and fury have possess’d the state, And fix’d the causes of a lasting hate. A bloody Hymen shall th’ alliance join Betwixt the Trojan and Ausonian line: But thou with speed to night and hell repair; For not the gods, nor angry Jove, will bear Thy lawless wand’ring walks in upper air. Leave what remains to me.” Saturnia said: The sullen fiend her sounding wings display’d, Unwilling left the light, and sought the nether shade.

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>undique ut auxilio veniunt; spargam arma per agros. <sup>552</sup>Tum contra Iuno; Terrorum et fraudis abunde est; <sup>553</sup>stant belli causae, pugnatur comminus armis, <sup>554</sup>quae fors prima dedit sanguis novus imbuit arma. <sup>555</sup>Talia coniugia et talis celebrent hymenaeos <sup>556</sup>egregium Veneris genus et rex ipse Latinus. <sup>557</sup>Te super aetherias errare licentius auras <sup>558</sup>haud pater ille velit, summi regnator Olympi: <sup>559</sup>cede locis; ego, siqua super fortuna laborum est, <sup>560</sup>ipsa regam. Talis dederat Saturnia voces. <sup>561</sup>Illa autem attollit stridentis anguibus alas <sup>562</sup>Cocytique petit sedem, supera ardua linquens. <sup>563</sup>Est locus Italiae medio sub montibus altis, <sup>564</sup>nobilis et fama multis memoratus in oris, <sup>565</sup>Amsancti valles: densis hunc frondibus atrum <sup>566</sup>urguet utrimque latus nemoris, medioque fragosus <sup>567</sup>dat sonitum saxis et torto vertice torrens. <sup>568</sup>Hic specus horrendum et saevi spiracula Ditis <sup>569</sup>monstrantur, ruptoque ingens Acheronte vorago <sup>570</sup>pestiferas aperit fauces, quis condita Erinys, <sup>571</sup>invisum numen, terras caelumque levabat. <sup>572</sup>Nec minus interea extremam Saturnia bello <sup>573</sup>imponit regina manum. Ruit omnis in urbem <sup>574</sup>pastorum ex acie numerus caesosque reportant <sup>575</sup>Almonem puerum foedatique ora Galaesi
translation.text:
In midst of Italy, well known to fame, There lies a lake, Amsanctus is the name, Below the lofty mounts: on either side Thick forests the forbidden entrance hide. Full in the centre of the sacred wood An arm arises of the Stygian flood, Which, breaking from beneath with bellowing sound, Whirls the black waves and rattling stones around. Here Pluto pants for breath from out his cell, And opens wide the grinning jaws of hell. To this infernal lake the Fury flies; Here hides her hated head, and frees the lab’ring skies.

Saturnian Juno now, with double care, Attends the fatal process of the war. The clowns, return’d, from battle bear the slain, Implore the gods, and to their king complain. The corps of Almon and the rest are shown; Shrieks, clamours, murmurs, fill the frighted town. Ambitious Turnus in the press appears, And, aggravating crimes, augments their fears; Proclaims his private injuries aloud, A solemn promise made, and disavow’d; A foreign son is sought, and a mix’d mungril brood. Then they, whose mothers, frantic with their fear, In woods and wilds the flags of Bacchus bear, And lead his dances with dishevel’d hair, Increase the clamour, and the war demand, (Such was Amata’s int’rest in the land,) Against the public sanctions of the peace, Against all omens of their ill success. With fates averse, the rout in arms resort, To force their monarch, and insult the court. But, like a rock unmov’d, a rock that braves The raging tempest and the rising waves, Propp’d on himself he stands; his solid sides Wash off the seaweeds, and the sounding tides: So stood the pious prince, unmov’d, and long Sustain’d the madness of the noisy throng. But, when he found that Juno’s pow’r prevail’d, And all the methods of cool counsel fail’d, He calls the gods to witness their offence, Disclaims the war, asserts his innocence. “Hurried by fate,” he cries, “and borne before A furious wind, we have the faithful shore. O more than madmen! you yourselves shall bear The guilt of blood and sacrilegious war: Thou, Turnus, shalt atone it by thy fate, And pray to Heav’n for peace, but pray too late. For me, my stormy voyage at an end, I to the port of death securely tend. The fun’ral pomp which to your kings you pay, Is all I want, and all you take away.” He said no more, but, in his walls confin’d, Shut out the woes which he too well divin’d Nor with the rising storm would vainly strive, But left the helm, and let the vessel drive.

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>implorantque deos obtestanturque Latinum. <sup>577</sup>Turnus adest medioque in crimine caedis et igni <sup>578</sup>terrorem ingeminat: Teucros in regna vocari, <sup>579</sup>stirpem admisceri Phrygiam, se limine pelli. <sup>580</sup>Tum quorum attonitae Baccho nemora avia matres <sup>581</sup>insultant thiasis (neque enim leve nomen Amatae), <sup>582</sup>undique collecti coeunt Martemque fatigant. <sup>583</sup>Ilicet infandum cuncti contra omina bellum, <sup>584</sup>contra fata deum perverso numine poscunt, <sup>585</sup>certatim regis circumstant tecta Latini. <sup>586</sup>Ille velut pelagi rupes immota resistit, <sup>587</sup>ut pelagi rupes magno veniente fragore, <sup>588</sup>quae sese multis circum latrantibus undis <sup>589</sup>mole tenet; scopuli nequiquam et spumea circum <sup>590</sup>saxa fremunt laterique inlisa refunditur alga. <sup>591</sup>Verum ubi nulla datur caecum exsuperare potestas <sup>592</sup>consilium et saevae nutu Iunonis eunt res, <sup>593</sup>multa deos aurasque pater testatus inanis: <sup>594</sup>Frangimur heu fatis, inquit,  ferimurque procella! <sup>595</sup>Ipsi has sacrilego pendetis sanguine poenas, <sup>596</sup>O miseri. Te, Turne, nefas, te triste manebit <sup>597</sup>supplicium, votisque deos venerabere seris. <sup>598</sup>Nam mihi parta quies, omnisque in limine portus; <sup>599</sup>funere felici spolior. Nec plura locutus <sup>600</sup>saepsit se tectis rerumque reliquit habenas.
translation.text: A solemn custom was observ’d of old, Which Latium held, and now the Romans hold, Their standard when in fighting fields they rear Against the fierce Hyrcanians, or declare The Scythian, Indian, or Arabian war; Or from the boasting Parthians would regain Their eagles, lost in Carrhae’s bloody plain. Two gates of steel (the name of Mars they bear, And still are worship’d with religious fear) Before his temple stand: the dire abode, And the fear’d issues of the furious god, Are fenc’d with brazen bolts; without the gates, The wary guardian Janus doubly waits. Then, when the sacred senate votes the wars, The Roman consul their decree declares, And in his robes the sounding gates unbars. The youth in military shouts arise, And the loud trumpets break the yielding skies. These rites, of old by sov’reign princes us’d, Were the king’s office; but the king refus’d, Deaf to their cries, nor would the gates unbar Of sacred peace, or loose th’ imprison’d war; But hid his head, and, safe from loud alarms, Abhorr’d the wicked ministry of arms. Then heav’n’s imperious queen shot down from high: At her approach the brazen hinges fly; The gates are forc’d, and ev’ry falling bar; And, like a tempest, issues out the war.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>Mos erat Hesperio in Latio, quem protinus urbes <sup>602</sup>Albanae coluere sacrum nunc maxima rerum <sup>603</sup>Roma colit, cum prima movent in proelia Martem, <sup>604</sup>sive Getis inferre manu lacrimabile bellum <sup>605</sup>Hyrcanisve Arabisve parant seu tendere ad Indos <sup>606</sup>Auroramque sequi Parthosque reposcere signa. <sup>607</sup>Sunt geminae belli portae (sic nomine dicunt) <sup>608</sup>religione sacrae et saevi formidine Martis; <sup>609</sup>centum aerei claudunt vectes aeternaque ferri <sup>610</sup>robora, nec custos absistit limine Ianus: <sup>611</sup>has, ubi certa sedet patribus sententia pugnae, <sup>612</sup>ipse Quirinali trabea cinctuque Gabino <sup>613</sup>insignis reserat stridentia limina consul, <sup>614</sup>ipse vocat pugnas; sequitur tum cetera pubes, <sup>615</sup>aereaque adsensu conspirant cornua rauco. <sup>616</sup>Hoc et tum Aeneadis indicere bella Latinus <sup>617</sup>more iubebatur tristisque recludere portas. <sup>618</sup>Abstinuit tactu pater aversusque refugit <sup>619</sup>foeda ministeria et caecis se condidit umbris. <sup>620</sup>Tum regina deum caelo delapsa morantis <sup>621</sup>impulit ipsa manu portas, et cardine verso <sup>622</sup>belli ferratos rumpit Saturnia postes. <sup>623</sup>Ardet inexcita Ausonia atque immobilis ante; <sup>624</sup>Pars pedes ire parat campis, pars arduus altis <sup>625</sup>pulverulentus equis furit; omnes arma requirunt.
translation.text:
The peaceful cities of th’ Ausonian shore, Lull’d in their ease, and undisturb’d before, Are all on fire; and some, with studious care, Their restiff steeds in sandy plains prepare; Some their soft limbs in painful marches try, And war is all their wish, and arms the gen’ral cry. Part scour the rusty shields with seam; and part New grind the blunted ax, and point the dart: With joy they view the waving ensigns fly, And hear the trumpet’s clangour pierce the sky. Five cities forge their arms: th’ Atinian pow’rs, Antemnae, Tibur with her lofty tow’rs, Ardea the proud, the Crustumerian town: All these of old were places of renown. Some hammer helmets for the fighting field; Some twine young sallows to support the shield; The croslet some, and some the cuishes mould, With silver plated, and with ductile gold. The rustic honours of the scythe and share Give place to swords and plumes, the pride of war. Old falchions are new temper’d in the fires; The sounding trumpet ev’ry soul inspires. The word is giv’n; with eager speed they lace The shining headpiece, and the shield embrace. The neighing steeds are to the chariot tied; The trusty weapon sits on ev’ry side.

And now the mighty labour is begun Ye Muses, open all your Helicon. Sing you the chiefs that sway’d th’ Ausonian land, Their arms, and armies under their command; What warriors in our ancient clime were bred; What soldiers follow’d, and what heroes led. For well you know, and can record alone, What fame to future times conveys but darkly down. Mezentius first appear’d upon the plain: Scorn sate upon his brows, and sour disdain, Defying earth and heav’n. Etruria lost, He brings to Turnus’ aid his baffled host. The charming Lausus, full of youthful fire, Rode in the rank, and next his sullen sire; To Turnus only second in the grace Of manly mien, and features of the face. A skilful horseman, and a huntsman bred, With fates averse a thousand men he led: His sire unworthy of so brave a son; Himself well worthy of a happier throne.
