translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>Pars levis clipeos et spicula lucida tergent <sup>627</sup>arvina pingui subiguntque in cote secures; <sup>628</sup>signaque ferre iuvat sonitusque audire tubarum. <sup>629</sup>Quinque adeo magnae positis incudibus urbes <sup>630</sup>tela novant, Atina potens Tiburque superbum, <sup>631</sup>Ardea Crustumerique et turrigerae Antemnae. <sup>632</sup>Tegmina tuta cavant capitum flectuntque salignas <sup>633</sup>umbonum cratis; alii thoracas aenos <sup>634</sup>aut levis ocreas lento ducunt argento; <sup>635</sup>vomeris huc et falcis honos, huc omnis aratri <sup>636</sup>cessit amor; recoquunt patrios fornacibus enses. <sup>637</sup>Classica iamque sonant; it bello tessera signum. <sup>638</sup>Hic galeam tectis trepidus rapit, ille frementis <sup>639</sup>ad iuga cogit equos clipeumque auroque trilicem <sup>640</sup>loricam induitur fidoque accingitur ense. <sup>641</sup>Pandite nunc Helicona, deae, cantusque movete, <sup>642</sup>qui bello exciti reges, quae quemque secutae <sup>643</sup>complerint campos acies, quibus Itala iam tum <sup>644</sup>floruerit terra alma viris, quibus arserit armis. <sup>645</sup>Et meministis enim, divae, et memorare potestis: <sup>646</sup>ad nos vix tenuis famae perlabitur aura. <sup>647</sup>Primus init bellum Tyrrhenis asper ab oris <sup>648</sup>contemptor divom Mezentius agminaque armat. <sup>649</sup>Filius huic iuxta Lausus, quo pulchrior alter <sup>650</sup>non fuit excepto Laurentis corpore Turni,
translation.text: Next Aventinus drives his chariot round The Latian plains, with palms and laurels crown’d. Proud of his steeds, he smokes along the field; His father’s hydra fills his ample shield: A hundred serpents hiss about the brims; The son of Hercules he justly seems By his broad shoulders and gigantic limbs; Of heav’nly part, and part of earthly blood, A mortal woman mixing with a god. For strong Alcides, after he had slain The triple Geryon, drove from conquer’d Spain His captive herds; and, thence in triumph led, On Tuscan Tiber’s flow’ry banks they fed. Then on Mount Aventine the son of Jove The priestess Rhea found, and forc’d to love. For arms, his men long piles and jav’lins bore; And poles with pointed steel their foes in battle gore. Like Hercules himself his son appears, In salvage pomp; a lion’s hide he wears; About his shoulders hangs the shaggy skin; The teeth and gaping jaws severely grin. Thus, like the god his father, homely dress’d, He strides into the hall, a horrid guest.

translation.original_text: <sup>651</sup>Lausus, equum domitor debellatorque ferarum, <sup>652</sup>ducit Agyllina nequiquam ex urbe secutos <sup>653</sup>mille viros, dignus, patriis qui laetior esset <sup>654</sup>imperiis et cui pater haud Mezentius esset. <sup>655</sup>Post hos insignem palma per gramina currum <sup>656</sup>victoresque ostentat equos satus Hercule pulchro <sup>657</sup>pulcher Aventinus, clipeoque insigne paternum <sup>658</sup>centum angues cinctamque gerit serpentibus hydram; <sup>659</sup>collis Aventini silva quem Rhea sacerdos <sup>660</sup>furtivum partu sub luminis edidit oras, <sup>661</sup>mixta deo mulier, postquam Laurentia victor <sup>662</sup>Geryone extincto Tirynthius attigit arva <sup>663</sup>Tyrrhenoque boves in flumine lavit Hiberas. <sup>664</sup>Pila manu saevosque gerunt in bella dolones <sup>665</sup>et tereti pugnant mucrone veruque Sabello. <sup>666</sup>Ipse pedes, tegumen torquens immane leonis, <sup>667</sup>terribili impexum saeta cum dentibus albis <sup>668</sup>indutus capiti, sic regia tecta subibat, <sup>669</sup>horridus, Herculeoque umeros innexus amictu. <sup>670</sup>Tum gemini fratres Tiburtia moenia linquunt, <sup>671</sup>fratris Tiburti dictam cognomine gentem, <sup>672</sup>Catillusque acerque Coras, Argiva iuventus, <sup>673</sup>et primam ante aciem densa inter tela feruntur: <sup>674</sup>ceu duo nubigenae cum vertice montis ab alto <sup>675</sup>descendunt centauri, Homolen Othrymque nivalem
translation.text:
Then two twin brothers from fair Tibur came, (Which from their brother Tiburs took the name,) Fierce Coras and Catillus, void of fear: Arm’d Argive horse they led, and in the front appear. Like cloud-born Centaurs, from the mountain’s height With rapid course descending to the fight; They rush along; the rattling woods give way; The branches bend before their sweepy sway.

Nor was Praeneste’s founder wanting there, Whom fame reports the son of Mulciber: Found in the fire, and foster’d in the plains, A shepherd and a king at once he reigns, And leads to Turnus’ aid his country swains. His own Praeneste sends a chosen band, With those who plow Saturnia’s Gabine land; Besides the succour which cold Anien yields, The rocks of Hernicus, and dewy fields, Anagnia fat, and Father Amasene— A num’rous rout, but all of naked men: Nor arms they wear, nor swords and bucklers wield, Nor drive the chariot thro’ the dusty field, But whirl from leathern slings huge balls of lead, And spoils of yellow wolves adorn their head; The left foot naked, when they march to fight, But in a bull’s raw hide they sheathe the right. Messapus next, (great Neptune was his sire,) Secure of steel, and fated from the fire, In pomp appears, and with his ardour warms A heartless train, unexercis’d in arms: The just Faliscans he to battle brings, And those who live where Lake Ciminius springs; And where Feronia’s grove and temple stands, Who till Fescennian or Flavinian lands. All these in order march, and marching sing The warlike actions of their sea-born king; Like a long team of snowy swans on high, Which clap their wings, and cleave the liquid sky, When, homeward from their wat’ry pastures borne, They sing, and Asia’s lakes their notes return. Not one who heard their music from afar, Would think these troops an army train’d to war, But flocks of fowl, that, when the tempests roar, With their hoarse gabbling seek the silent shore.

translation.original_text: <sup>676</sup>linquentes cursu rapido; dat euntibus ingens <sup>677</sup>silva locum et magno cedunt virgulta fragore. <sup>678</sup>Nec Praenestinae fundator defuit urbis, <sup>679</sup>Volcano genitum pecora inter agrestia regem <sup>680</sup>inventumque focis omnis quem credidit aetas <sup>681</sup>Caeculus. Hunc late legio comitatur agrestis: <sup>682</sup>quique altum Praeneste viri quique arva Gabinae <sup>683</sup>Iunonis gelidumque Anienem et roscida rivis <sup>684</sup>Hernica saxa colunt, quos dives Anagnia pascit, <sup>685</sup>quos, Amasene pater. Non illis omnibus arma, <sup>686</sup>nec clipei currusve sonant: pars maxima glandes <sup>687</sup>liventis plumbi spargit, pars spicula gestat <sup>688</sup>bina manu, fulvosque lupi de pelle galeros <sup>689</sup>tegmen habent capiti, vestigia nuda sinistri <sup>690</sup>instituere pedis, crudus tegit altera pero. <sup>691</sup>At Messapus, equum domitor, Neptunia proles, <sup>692</sup>quem neque fas igni cuiquam nec sternere ferro, <sup>693</sup>iam pridem resides populos desuetaque bello <sup>694</sup>agmina in arma vocat subito ferrumque retractat. <sup>695</sup>Hi Fescenninas acies Aequosque Faliscos. <sup>696</sup>Hi Soractis habent arces Flaviniaque arva <sup>697</sup>et Cimini cum monte lacum lucosque Capenos. <sup>698</sup>Ibant aequati numero regemque canebant, <sup>699</sup>ceu quondam nivei liquida inter nubila cycni, <sup>700</sup>cum sese e pastu referunt et longa canoros
translation.text: Then Clausus came, who led a num’rous band Of troops embodied from the Sabine land, And, in himself alone, an army brought. ’Twas he, the noble Claudian race begot, The Claudian race, ordain’d, in times to come, To share the greatness of imperial Rome. He led the Cures forth, of old renown, Mutuscans from their olive-bearing town, And all th’ Eretian pow’rs; besides a band That follow’d from Velinum’s dewy land, And Amiternian troops, of mighty fame, And mountaineers, that from Severus came, And from the craggy cliffs of Tetrica, And those where yellow Tiber takes his way, And where Himella’s wanton waters play. Casperia sends her arms, with those that lie By Fabaris, and fruitful Foruli: The warlike aids of Horta next appear, And the cold Nursians come to close the rear, Mix’d with the natives born of Latine blood, Whom Allia washes with her fatal flood. Not thicker billows beat the Libyan main, When pale Orion sets in wintry rain; Nor thicker harvests on rich Hermus rise, Or Lycian fields, when Phoebus burns the skies, Than stand these troops: their bucklers ring around; Their trampling turns the turf, and shakes the solid ground.

translation.original_text: <sup>701</sup>dant per colla modos, sonat amnis et Asia longe <sup>702</sup>pulsa palus. <sup>703</sup>Nec quisquam aeratas acies ex agmine tanto <sup>704</sup>misceri putet, aeriam sed gurgite ab alto <sup>705</sup>urgueri volucrum raucarum ad litora nubem. <sup>706</sup>Ecce Sabinorum prisco de sanguine magnum <sup>707</sup>agmen agens Clausus magnique ipse agminis instar, <sup>708</sup>Claudia nunc a quo diffunditur et tribus et gens <sup>709</sup>per Latium, postquam in partem data Roma Sabinis. <sup>710</sup>Una ingens Amiterna cohors priscique Quirites, <sup>711</sup>Ereti manus omnis oliviferaeque Mutuscae; <sup>712</sup>qui Nomentum urbem, qui Rosea rura Velini, <sup>713</sup>qui Tetricae horrentis rupes montemque Severum <sup>714</sup>Casperiamque colunt Forulosque et flumen Himellae, <sup>715</sup>qui Tiberim Fabarimque bibunt, quos frigida misit <sup>716</sup>Nursia, et Hortinae classes populique Latini, <sup>717</sup>quosque secans infaustum interluit Allia nomen: <sup>718</sup>quam multi Libyco volvuntur marmore fluctus <sup>719</sup>saevus ubi Orion hibernis conditur undis; <sup>720</sup>vel cum sole novo densae torrentur aristae <sup>721</sup>aut Hermi campo aut Lyciae flaventibus arvis. <sup>722</sup>Scuta sonant pulsuque pedum conterrita tellus. <sup>723</sup>Hinc Agamemnonius, Troiani nominis hostis, <sup>724</sup>curru iungit Halaesus equos Turnoque ferocis <sup>725</sup>mille rapit populos, vertunt felicia Baccho
translation.text: High in his chariot then Halesus came, A foe by birth to Troy’s unhappy name: From Agamemnon born—to Turnus’ aid A thousand men the youthful hero led, Who till the Massic soil, for wine renown’d, And fierce Auruncans from their hilly ground, And those who live by Sidicinian shores, And where with shoaly fords Vulturnus roars, Cales’ and Osca’s old inhabitants, And rough Saticulans, inur’d to wants: Light demi-lances from afar they throw, Fasten’d with leathern thongs, to gall the foe. Short crooked swords in closer fight they wear; And on their warding arm light bucklers bear.

translation.original_text: <sup>726</sup>Massica qui rastris et quos de collibus altis <sup>727</sup>Aurunci misere patres, Sidicinaque iuxta <sup>728</sup>aequora quique Cales linquunt, amnisque vadosi <sup>729</sup>accola Volturni, pariterque Saticulus asper <sup>730</sup>Oscorumque manus. Teretes sunt aclydes illis <sup>731</sup>tela, sed haec lento mos est aptare flagello; <sup>732</sup>laevas caetra tegit, falcati comminus enses. <sup>733</sup>Nec tu carminibus nostris indictus abibis, <sup>734</sup>Oebale, quem generasse Telon Sebethide nympha <sup>735</sup>fertur, Teleboum Capreas cum regna teneret, <sup>736</sup>iam senior; patriis sed non et filius arvis <sup>737</sup>contentus late iam tum dicione premebat <sup>738</sup>Sarrastis populos et quae rigat aequora Sarnus <sup>739</sup>quique Rufras Batulumque tenent atque arva Celemnae <sup>740</sup>et quos maliferae despectant moenia Abellae, <sup>741</sup>Teutonico ritu soliti torquere cateias, <sup>742</sup>tegmina quis capitum raptus de subere cortex, <sup>743</sup>aerataeque micant peltae, micat aereus ensis. <sup>744</sup>Et te montosae misere in proelia Nersae, <sup>745</sup>Ufens, insignem fama et felicibus armis; <sup>746</sup>horrida praecipue cui gens adsuetaque multo <sup>747</sup>venatu nemorum, duris Aequicula glaebis. <sup>748</sup>Armati terram exercent, semperque recentis <sup>749</sup>convectare iuvat praedas et vivere rapto. <sup>750</sup>Quin et Marruvia venit de gente sacerdos,
translation.text:
Nor Oebalus, shalt thou be left unsung, From nymph Semethis and old Telon sprung, Who then in Teleboan Capri reign’d; But that short isle th’ ambitious youth disdain’d, And o’er Campania stretch’d his ample sway, Where swelling Sarnus seeks the Tyrrhene sea; O’er Batulum, and where Abella sees, From her high tow’rs, the harvest of her trees. And these (as was the Teuton use of old) Wield brazen swords, and brazen bucklers hold; Sling weighty stones, when from afar they fight; Their casques are cork, a covering thick and light.

Next these in rank, the warlike Ufens went, And led the mountain troops that Nursia sent. The rude Equicolae his rule obey’d; Hunting their sport, and plund’ring was their trade. In arms they plow’d, to battle still prepar’d: Their soil was barren, and their hearts were hard.

translation.original_text: <sup>751</sup>fronde super galeam et felici comptus oliva. <sup>752</sup>Archippi regis missu, fortissimus Umbro, <sup>753</sup>vipereo generi et graviter spirantibus hydris <sup>754</sup>spargere qui somnos cantuque manuque solebat <sup>755</sup>mulcebatque iras et morsus arte levabat. <sup>756</sup>Sed non Dardaniae medicari cuspidis ictum <sup>757</sup>evaluit, neque eum iuvere in volnera cantus <sup>758</sup>somniferi et Marsis quaesitae montibus herbae. <sup>759</sup>Te nemus Angitiae, vitrea te Fucinus unda, <sup>760</sup>te liquidi flevere lacus. <sup>761</sup>Ibat et Hippolyti proles pulcherrima bello, <sup>762</sup>Virbius, insignem quem mater Aricia misit, <sup>763</sup>eductum Egeriae lucis umentia circum <sup>764</sup>litora, pinguis ubi et placabilis ara Dianae. <sup>765</sup>Namque ferunt fama Hippolytum, postquam arte novercae <sup>766</sup>occiderit patriasque explerit sanguine poenas <sup>767</sup>turbatis distractus equis, ad sidera rursus <sup>768</sup>aetheria et superas caeli venisse sub auras, <sup>769</sup>Paeoniis revocatum herbis et amore Dianae. <sup>770</sup>Tum pater omnipotens, aliquem indignatus ab umbris <sup>771</sup>mortalem infernis ad lumina surgere vitae, <sup>772</sup>ipse repertorem medicinae talis et artis <sup>773</sup>fulmine Phoebigenam Stygias detrusit ad undas. <sup>774</sup>At Trivia Hippolytum secretis alma recondit <sup>775</sup>sedibus et nymphae Egeriae nemorique relegat,
translation.text: Umbro the priest the proud Marrubians led, By King Archippus sent to Turnus’ aid, And peaceful olives crown’d his hoary head. His wand and holy words, the viper’s rage, And venom’d wounds of serpents could assuage. He, when he pleas’d with powerful juice to steep Their temples, shut their eyes in pleasing sleep. But vain were Marsian herbs, and magic art, To cure the wound giv’n by the Dardan dart: Yet his untimely fate th’ Angitian woods In sighs remurmur’d to the Fucine floods.

translation.original_text: <sup>776</sup>solus ubi in silvis Italis ignobilis aevom <sup>777</sup>exigeret versoque ubi nomine Virbius esset. <sup>778</sup>Unde etiam templo Triviae lucisque sacratis <sup>779</sup>cornipedes arcentur equi, quod litore currum <sup>780</sup>et iuvenem monstris pavidi effudere marinis. <sup>781</sup>Filius ardentis haud setius aequore campi <sup>782</sup>exercebat equos curruque in bella ruebat. <sup>783</sup>Ipse inter primos praestanti corpore Turnus <sup>784</sup>vertitur arma tenens et toto vertice supra est. <sup>785</sup>Cui triplici crinita iuba galea alta Chimaeram <sup>786</sup>sustinet, Aetnaeos efflantem faucibus ignis: <sup>787</sup>tam magis illa fremens et tristibus effera flammis, <sup>788</sup>quam magis effuso crudescunt sanguine pugnae. <sup>789</sup>At levem clipeum sublatis cornibus Io <sup>790</sup>auro insignibat, iam saetis obsita, iam bos <sup>791</sup>(argumentum ingens), et custos virginis Argus <sup>792</sup>caelataque amnem fundens pater Inachus urna. <sup>793</sup>Insequitur nimbus peditum clipeataque totis <sup>794</sup>agmina densentur campis, Argivaque pubes <sup>795</sup>Auruncaeque manus, Rutuli veteresque Sicani <sup>796</sup>et Sacranae acies et picti scuta Labici; <sup>797</sup>qui saltus, Tiberine, tuos sacrumque Numici <sup>798</sup>litus arant Rutulosque exercent vomere colles <sup>799</sup>Circaeumque iugum, quis Iuppiter Anxurus arvis <sup>800</sup>praesidet et viridi gaudens Feronia luco;
translation.text:
The son of fam’d Hippolytus was there, Fam’d as his sire, and, as his mother, fair; Whom in Egerian groves Aricia bore, And nurs’d his youth along the marshy shore, Where great Diana’s peaceful altars flame, In fruitful fields; and Virbius was his name. Hippolytus, as old records have said, Was by his stepdam sought to share her bed; But, when no female arts his mind could move, She turn’d to furious hate her impious love. Torn by wild horses on the sandy shore, Another’s crimes th’ unhappy hunter bore, Glutting his father’s eyes with guiltless gore. But chaste Diana, who his death deplor’d, With Aesculapian herbs his life restor’d. Then Jove, who saw from high, with just disdain, The dead inspir’d with vital breath again, Struck to the centre, with his flaming dart, Th’ unhappy founder of the godlike art. But Trivia kept in secret shades alone Her care, Hippolytus, to fate unknown; And call’d him Virbius in th’ Egerian grove, Where then he liv’d obscure, but safe from Jove. For this, from Trivia’s temple and her wood Are coursers driv’n, who shed their master’s blood, Affrighted by the monsters of the flood. His son, the second Virbius, yet retain’d His father’s art, and warrior steeds he rein’d.

Amid the troops, and like the leading god, High o’er the rest in arms the graceful Turnus rode: A triple of plumes his crest adorn’d, On which with belching flames Chimaera burn’d: The more the kindled combat rises high’r, The more with fury burns the blazing fire. Fair Io grac’d his shield; but Io now With horns exalted stands, and seems to low— A noble charge! Her keeper by her side, To watch her walks, his hundred eyes applied; And on the brims her sire, the wat’ry god, Roll’d from a silver urn his crystal flood. A cloud of foot succeeds, and fills the fields With swords, and pointed spears, and clatt’ring shields; Of Argives, and of old Sicanian bands, And those who plow the rich Rutulian lands; Auruncan youth, and those Sacrana yields, And the proud Labicans, with painted shields, And those who near Numician streams reside, And those whom Tiber’s holy forests hide, Or Circe’s hills from the main land divide; Where Ufens glides along the lowly lands, Or the black water of Pomptina stands.

translation.original_text: <sup>801</sup>qua Saturae iacet atra palus gelidusque per imas <sup>802</sup>quaerit iter vallis atque in mare conditur Ufens. <sup>803</sup>Hos super advenit Volsca de gente Camilla <sup>804</sup>agmen agens equitum et florentis aere catervas, <sup>805</sup>bellatrix, non illa colo calathisve Minervae <sup>806</sup>femineas adsueta manus, sed proelia virgo <sup>807</sup>dura pati cursuque pedum praevertere ventos. <sup>808</sup>Illa vel intactae segetis per summa volaret <sup>809</sup>gramina nec teneras cursu laesisset aristas, <sup>810</sup>vel mare per medium fluctu suspensa tumenti <sup>811</sup>ferret iter celeris nec tingueret aequore plantas. <sup>812</sup>Illam omnis tectis agrisque effusa iuventus <sup>813</sup>turbaque miratur matrum et prospectat euntem, <sup>814</sup>attonitis inhians animis, ut regius ostro <sup>815</sup>velet honos levis umeros, ut fibula crinem <sup>816</sup>auro internectat, Lyciam ut gerat ipsa pharetram <sup>817</sup>et pastoralem praefixa cuspide myrtum.
translation.text: Last, from the Volscians fair Camilla came, And led her warlike troops, a warrior dame; Unbred to spinning, in the loom unskill’d, She chose the nobler Pallas of the field. Mix’d with the first, the fierce Virago fought, Sustain’d the toils of arms, the danger sought, Outstripp’d the winds in speed upon the plain, Flew o’er the fields, nor hurt the bearded grain: She swept the seas, and, as she skimm’d along, Her flying feet unbath’d on billows hung. Men, boys, and women, stupid with surprise, Where’er she passes, fix their wond’ring eyes: Longing they look, and, gaping at the sight, Devour her o’er and o’er with vast delight; Her purple habit sits with such a grace On her smooth shoulders, and so suits her face; Her head with ringlets of her hair is crown’d, And in a golden caul the curls are bound. She shakes her myrtle jav’lin; and, behind, Her Lycian quiver dances in the wind.
